entries.title: White Sox rookie Noah Schultz shines in second start, Murakami homers again in win
entries.summary: <p>WEST SACRAMENTO, Calif. — The lowly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> were due for a bang-up afternoon, and they got one Sunday against the Athletics.</p><p>There was Japanese phenom Munetaka Murakami admiring his towering home run for a third straight game. There were Colson Montgomery and Miguel Vargas tagging their own impressive blasts, plus Chase Meidroth scratching out two hits.</p><p>Nevermind a few late bullpen hiccups, because most importantly, there was left-hander Noah Schultz dealing through five strong innings in the second start of his career — and letting his 97-mph sinker answer any lingering questions about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/14/noah-schultz-white-sox-mlb-debut" target="_blank" ><u>his shaky Chicago debut</u></a> last week.</p><p>The Aurora native gave up a solo home run, a walk and nothing else while striking out six over five innings against a powerful Athletics lineup en route to a 7-4 win. He threw 82 pitches, identical to his first outing last Tuesday at Rate Field that was spoiled by a three-run first inning including a two-run throwing error from the 6-10 Schultz.</p><p>Schultz was charged with an error in West Sacramento, too, after he couldn’t handle Murakami’s toss while covering first on a grounder. But there wasn’t any hint of jitters from the Oswego East product who acknowledged he had to shake them off the first time he took the mound.</p><p>Schultz, who served up the first home run of his career to A’s third baseman Darell Hernaiz, had the benefit of healthy run support from an offense that has found its footing on the West Coast.</p><p>Catcher Edgar Quero knocked a sacrifice fly in the first inning, and the Sox continued feasting on A’s starter Jeffrey Springs in the second with a solo homer from Derek Hill and Vargas’ two-run blast.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Munetaka Murakami has hit 8 home runs this season.<br><br>This is the SECOND time he's homered in 3 straight games! <a href="https://t.co/KsSpNTOxOU">https://t.co/KsSpNTOxOU</a> <a href="https://t.co/rEz6I0pRH4">pic.twitter.com/rEz6I0pRH4</a></p>— MLB (@MLB) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB/status/2045977413570470303?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 19, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The Sox then staked Schultz to a 7-1 lead in the fifth inning with Murakami’s latest eye-popping blast, a two-run shot sent sailing at 114 mph off the bat and 415 deep past the outfield lawn at Sutter Health Park. It marked his eighth U.S. homer and the second three-game homer streak of his budding career, following his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/01/munetaka-murakami-chicago-white-sox-debut" target="_blank" ><u>torrid opening weekend</u></a> with the Sox.</p><p>Montgomery followed up with his own 419-foot shot to straightaway center field for his fifth homer of the season and second in as many days.</p><p>But after watching a five-run lead evaporate on Saturday, Sox relievers let the Athletics chip away in the seventh inning. The A’s tagged Grant Taylor for two runs in the seventh inning on a double from second baseman Zack Gelof, then plated another on a wild pitch from Jordan Leasure.</p><p>Reliever Bryan Hudson escaped the eighth after giving up a double, and closer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/17/white-sox-seranthony-dominguez-anthony-kay-athletics?99" target="_blank" ><u>Seranthony Dominguez</u></a> worked around a walk and a hit batter to seal the win. </p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Kamala Harris says in Chicago that Trump ‘has made America weaker’
entries.summary: <p>During an appearance Sunday in Chicago, former Vice President Kamala Harris said President Trump’s war on Iran has “made America weaker.”</p><p>“He is the first president, Republican or Democrat since World War II, to not concern himself [with] the importance of America's alliances, our friendships and America's responsibility to uphold standards such as sovereignty and territorial integrity,” Harris told a crowd at the Power Rising Summit, a convening of Black women harnessing their collective wisdom to create an actionable agenda.</p><p>The conversation happened at the Soul Sunday Brunch on the final day of the three-day event at the downtown Hilton Chicago. She was interviewed by summit founder Leah Daughtry, CEO of the 2008 Democratic National Convention Committee.</p><p>Harris said the country’s involvement in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/03/01/chicagoans-iran-middle-east-ties-worry-united-states-israel-continue-bombing" target="_blank" ><u>war in Iran</u></a>, a “war the American people don’t want,” is a result of Trump “just getting persuaded by” Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netenayhu.</p><p>She said Trump’s actions have “caused us to become more unreliable and to lose our influence to then be able — imperfect, though, we certainly are flawed — to talk about matters of human rights around the world.”</p><p>The Biden-Harris administration was not free of criticism regarding human rights. In March, <a class="Link" href="https://www.axios.com/2026/02/22/dnc-2024-autopsy-harris-gaza" target="_blank" ><u>Axios</u></a> reported that a hidden DNC report found that the Biden administration’s position on the war in Gaza cost Harris the 2024 election.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" presidential election after a similar percentage supported former President Joe Biden and Harris as vice president in 2020, according to the Pew Research Center.</p><p>Harris said political She met a Black single mother in Jackson, Miss., and went grocery shopping with her. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez listens to Harris speak during the “Power Rising" summit." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/82639b6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7376x4140+0+390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fc6%2Fd3fb6cc749a39292a56db71601c4%2Fharris-260420-007.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/257b197/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7376x4140+0+390/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fc6%2Fd3fb6cc749a39292a56db71601c4%2Fharris-260420-007.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/82639b6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7376x4140+0+390/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F25%2Fc6%2Fd3fb6cc749a39292a56db71601c4%2Fharris-260420-007.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>U.S. Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez listens to Harris speak during the “Power Rising” summit.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Looking ahead to this November’s midterm elections, Harris said “it’s gonna be so difficult” for the Democrats to win, but that the path includes “a vision for affordable child care, affordable health care, affordable housing and … a vision that is about actually getting this stuff done.”</p><p>Harris criticized leaders, including Democrats, who are in office and "preserving their title at the expense of actually getting anything done.</p><p>“And then when you try to look at what they've actually done to move the needle, you're left wanting,” she said. “And so part of the work has to be accountability.”</p><p>In 2028 and beyond, Harris said she believes that “America is, can be and will be ready for a woman to be president of the United States.” Harris <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/2026/04/10/politics/kamala-harris-2028-presidential-election" target="_blank" ><u>said earlier this month</u></a> that she’s “thinking about” another run. If elected, she would become the first woman to hold the office, adding to a list of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2020/8/11/21363819/biden-picks-kamala-harris-for-running-mate" target="_blank" ><u>firsts</u></a> throughout her career: The first Black person and first woman to be elected as California’s Attorney General, and the first woman and first Black person to be Vice President of the United States.</p><p>"There are still certain myths about what a woman can and cannot do in spite of what she actually does,” Harris said. “My mother sat me down to say that, for me to get ready for what it would mean to run, and she followed that up by saying it's a myth.”<br></p>

entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, April 20, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Caution! Avoid shopping (except food and gas) and important decisions ALL DAY today. The moon is in Gemini.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You are an action-oriented sign who likes to move fast. Therefore, be careful today because you might have clever ideas and long-range plans that excite you. You want to discuss them and make things happen. Know this: All day today is a moon alert. (See above.)</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a tricky day. entries.title: Schiller Park woman found dead in Chicago River on South Side
entries.summary: <p>A woman was found dead in the Chicago River Sunday morning on the Near South Side. </p><p>Maria Jose Casas Fabela, 20, was pulled from the water in the 2200 block of South Grove Avenue around 8:05 a.m. Sunday, Chicago police and the Cook County medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Casas Fabela, of Northwest suburban Schiller Park, was dead at the scene, police said. An autopsy is scheduled that will determine the cause and manner of her death. </p><p>Area detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Red pickup sought in fatal Belmont Cragin hit-and-run
entries.summary: <p>Police are looking for the driver of a red pickup truck that fatally hit a 65-year-old woman walking in a crosswalk and fled the scene, Sunday night in Belmont Cragin. </p><p>The woman, 65, was in the crosswalk in the 3100 block of North Central Avenue around 9:30 p.m. when a red pickup truck going south struck the woman and continued south, Chicago police said. </p><p>The woman, whose name was not released, was pronounced dead at the scene, police said. </p><p>Major Accident detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Man spends money meant for daughters on himself
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My oldest granddaughter will be looking at colleges soon. My son and her mother are divorced. My parents gave my son (their grandson) money to put away in accounts for his daughters' college. Instead, he helped himself to that money before the divorce. When his ex-wife told me about it, I confronted him. I decided not to tell my parents about it because I was afraid it might shock them into a stroke or heart attack. They are in their 90s.</p><p>I have put away some money to help pay for college, but it won't cover the costs. I have kept this a secret because I don't trust my son to manage it. When I asked him how he planned to help pay for college, he said he would use the money he now pays for child support. I don't find that answer satisfactory.</p><p>I feel like a failure for having raised a son who would take money that belongs to his kids. My granddaughter is upset and feels her father has ruined her future. She also thinks I should tell my parents that he stole the money they intended for her. What do you think I should do? — <i>MORTIFIED IN MASSACHUSETTS</i></p><p><b>DEAR MORTIFIED:</b> I suspect that you didn't tell your parents what your son did because you are ashamed of how he has turned out. That he would steal from his daughters is despicable. I don't blame your granddaughter for being angry with her father for what he did. I doubt that your parents knowing the truth will affect their health. Inform them about what happened to the college fund, in case they have been planning to leave anything in their estate to their grandson.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have conflicting feelings regarding Valentine's Day. I believe it is a celebration for couples rather than co-workers. My husband's office staff (eight young women under the age of 30) insist on celebrating Valentine's Day with decorations on all office doors, complete with hearts and cupids. They have a catered lunch with specialty treats of chocolate-covered strawberries, fudge hearts and the customary heart candies that read "Be Mine," "I Love You", etc.</p><p>My husband has been with this company for 30 years, and we've been together for 15 of them, but this Valentine's Day celebration began only four years ago. I am 65 and have worked 20-plus years for a Fortune 500 corporate office and NEVER has Valentine's Day been celebrated in the office. Christmas, yes. Fourth of July, yes. But Valentine's Day? Am I just old and cranky? This has been a source of contention between my husband and me since it began. — <i>NOT LOVIN' THAT IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR NOT LOVIN':</b> I have heard of some people loving their jobs; perhaps this is their way of showing it. It may also be the employer's way of showing appreciation for their (largely female) support staff. Unless you have gotten a "whiff" that there is more than that going on, let it ride.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>What teens need to know about sex, drugs, AIDS and getting along with peers and parents is in "What Every Teen Should Know." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Teen Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>

entries.title: How to spot health misinformation that's fueling rise in disease outbreaks
entries.summary: <p>Outbreaks of measles, meningitis and RSV have surged across the U.S. as vaccination rates continue to decline. The spike in these vaccine-preventable diseases underscores the real, and sometimes deadly, consequences of public health misinformation and the urgent need to address it before it spreads further.</p><p>Confronting misinformation at this scale requires more than defensively debunking each inaccurate claim as it comes up. A more durable approach is to equip people with the tools to recognize misleading arguments before they take hold. This approach, called “pre-bunking,” helps identify common patterns used to spread misleading claims.</p><p>To understand why this approach matters, it helps to understand how misinformation — and sometimes deliberately misleading disinformation — works.</p><p>Part of the challenge is misleading health information is often persuasive by design. It draws on familiar ways of thinking: mental shortcuts that can make claims feel reasonable and credible, even when they are not supported by evidence. Many people encounter and internalize these claims online well before speaking with a health care provider or hearing from a public health professional.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-430000" name="module-430000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>One of the most common misinformation tactics is the “appeal to nature,” the assumption that something is inherently safer or better simply because it is described as natural. Yet nature includes both beneficial and harmful elements. Consider that contagious viruses, toxic arsenic and poisonous plants are natural, but that doesn’t mean they are good for you.</p><p>By contrast, vaccines are the result of rigorous scientific innovation; many have been used for decades and are responsible for saving millions of lives. The relevant question is not whether something is “natural” but whether it is proven to be safe and effective.</p><p>Another common tactic presents a false choice. Complex public health issues are framed as “either-or-decisions." Suggesting that children need either vaccines or a healthy diet is one example of this. In reality, health protection is not a single intervention but a combination of complementary strategies. As a father myself, both vaccines and a balanced diet are high on my priority list when I think of ways to keep my kids healthy.</p><p>In other cases, arguments shift attention from evidence altogether and toward undermining trust, questioning the motives of scientists or invoking generalized concerns about industry influence. While scrutiny and accountability are important, these arguments often distract from the central question: What does the evidence show?</p><p>Some claims rely on an appeal to “common sense,” implying that complex health challenges must have simple explanations. But public health requires careful study, large datasets and sustained analysis to understand patterns and determine what works.</p><p>At a time when misinformation can spread as quickly as a virus, being able to evaluate claims and recognize misinformation is critical. Even a brief pause can make a meaningful difference. Before accepting or sharing a health claim, it is worth asking a few basic questions: What is the source? Is there credible evidence? Does the argument rely on a familiar shortcut or simplicity in reasoning rather than verifiable facts?</p><p>Both our individual and community health rely on our ability to tell the difference between what feels true and what is rooted in scientific evidence. By strengthening our pre-bunking skills, we can better label misinformation and stop its spread before it does more harm.</p><p>In my role at the Cook County Department of Public Health, I see the consequences of misinformation firsthand, where declining vaccination rates have left more than 13,000 children in suburban Cook County unprotected against measles. Sadly, that’s double the number from a decade ago. </p><p>Reversing this trend is a top priority. By strengthening public trust, promoting accurate information and supporting families in making informed decisions based on facts, we strive to protect residents from diseases we already have the tools to stop.</p><p><i>Dr. Kiran Joshi is chief operating officer of the Cook County Department of Public Health.</i> </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-960000" name="module-960000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: Review: Writers Theatre’s ‘Job’ is a nail-biting thrill ride about Big Tech and its irreversible impact
entries.summary: <p>Max Wolf Friedlich’s “Job” takes on a lot in its harrowing 85-minute run time. Through two people on one set, the drama digs into mental illness, the generational abyss between boomers and millennials, virulent misogyny and Big Tech’s indelible role in promulgating all of the above. Often incongruously funny, “Job” is infused with a looming, ever-intensifying dread that feels all too timely.</p><p>At heart, “Job” is shaped around a high-tech, grotesque take on the “trolley question” — whether killing one person can be justified if it can save many people. Initially murky stakes turn nightmarishly clear through a series of harrowing scenes that ratchet up the tension in a vice grip.</p><p>Director David Esbjornson paces production like a runaway train that picks up steam as it hurtles toward a conclusion that could mean death, prison, institutionalization or what passes for a happy(ish) ending in a broken world where snuff films and torture porn are accessible to anyone with internet access.</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="4.0" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> ‘Job’ </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c90000" name="module-c90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through June 14<br><b>Where:</b> Writers Theatre, 325 Tudor Court, Glencoe<br><b>Tickets:</b> $50 and up <br><b>Run time:</b> 85 minutes<br><b>More info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.writerstheatre.org/events/job" target="_blank" >writerstheatre.org</a><br></div> </div><p>Friedlich begins by throwing the audience off balance. Through a series of stuttering light flashes, we see a millennial woman with a gun pointed at a boomer man. There’s little dialogue, just a dissonant blare of sound and a blazing glare of light as the tableaus continue. The dots are gradually filled in.</p><p>Jane (Rae Gray) has been sent to see a therapist, Loyd (Christopher Donahue), after having a screaming meltdown at work that went viral. So Goog — Jane vehemently cuts Loyd off when he attempts to mention her tech giant employer — sends Jane to therapy. She needs Loyd’s clearance to return to work. Losing her job, Jane asserts with ice-cold clarity, would be the single worst thing that could happen to her. By the end, she’ll learn there is always something worse than the worst thing you can imagine.</p><p>Jane’s job would crush most people. She’s in charge of removing heinous content from the web: torture porn, snuff films, necrophila, extreme self-harm and the like. She’s devoted her life to chopping off the head of a Hydra that will only grow a hundredfold by the time her next shift begins.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rae Gray portrays Jane, an employee sent to therapy by her employer after her meltdown at work went viral, in “Job.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Hugo Hentoff</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Many of the monstrous revelations made explicit in the play’s latter half are subtly implicit in the first half. Similar to the 1999 film “The Sixth Sense,” “Job” will make you want to go back and parse the therapist’s every sentence with the benefit of hindsight.</p><p>Things seem straightforward — Loyd’s college days at Berkeley, the daughter who motivates his practice, his jewelry-making hobby, his near-Luddite aversion to social media — and are not entirely what they seem. Nothing is straightforward in this alleged therapy session. After the final blackout, retrospect warps nearly every seemingly banal line into a reminder of the banality of evil. </p><p>Gray’s nuanced, rapid-fire performance is double-sided: Jane could be seriously ill. Or she could simply be having a rational response to a profoundly irrational world. Either way, Gray puts on a clinic in sardonic delivery. If boomers really wanted to do something to save the planet for future generations, they’d kill their parents and redistribute all the inheritances, Jane says at one point. It’s not entirely clear if this is a modest satirical proposal or an earnest suggestion. The ambiguity is a testament to Gray’s prowess.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Christopher Donahue is Loyd, a therapist working with Jane, in “Job.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Hugo Hentoff</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Donahue is also adept at emotional undercurrents. He can move from terror to relief in the space of two guttural “huhs.” He makes Loyd’s compassionate competence believable, but there’s no denying that something murky and vaguely fetid lurks below. Donahue makes the dankness rise incrementally to the surface, like a drowned person gradually washing up on the beach.</p><p>Jack Magaw’s understated set gives us a therapist’s office that’s aggressively analogue. There are no laptops. There is nothing plugged into the sole outlet on view. The furniture is antique-y and heavy — that weight coming into play in a pivotal scene that reveals either the depths of Jane’s mental health struggles or the savvy chess maneuver of someone who is fully in control.</p><p>Light (James F. Ingalls) and sound (Willow James and Christopher Kriz) are crucial storytelling elements beyond that memorable opening. Together, they create moments of cacophony that serve as unsettling harbingers.</p><p>“Job” is a nail-biter, right up until the end. Big Tech has defined both Loyd and Jane’s lives with profound, irreversible impact. It’s not hard — although it is terrifying — to extrapolate its impact on all of us.</p>

entries.title: Rude cabdrivers need etiquette training
entries.summary: <p>In reference to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/13/chicago-taxicab-fares-increase-20-percent-lyft-uber" target="_blank" >assisting cabdrivers by raising fees by 20%</a>, I'd like to offer a constructive suggestion: How about using that money to conduct seminars on professionalism, particularly for cabdrivers stationed at O'Hare Airport?</p><p>My last three cab rides from O'Hare were as follows:</p><p>One driver engaged in a very loud conversation with someone on his cellphone while driving. When I tried getting his attention to provide further directions, the driver snapped at me: "What? What? I am on the phone!"</p><p>Another driver, after I told him that my ride was only a "short one" from the airport to a nearby suburb, dropped the F bomb at me and then rushed to complain to the city employee who was directing visitors to other cabs.</p><p>My last driver had an open bag of peanuts on his lap and ate them while talking on his cellphone. He also went over the speed limit and bumped his wheels into wall protectors (twice) as he jumped into a shoulder lane.</p><p>Cabdrivers are probably the first "official greeters" of out-of-town visitors to Chicago. They are our ambassadors.</p><p>The behaviors of the three drivers mentioned above were disgraceful. They set a horrid example of hospitality.</p><p>Although I finally got fed up and filed an actual complaint against the last gentleman (the peanut man), I have no idea if there was any follow-through. For all I know, he is still allowed to pick up passengers at O'Hare.</p><p>Charity, they say, starts at home. So does professionalism. The city and its drivers need to get their act together.</p><p><i>Bill Dal Cerro,</i> <i>Edison Park</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-710000" name="module-710000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>These proposed laws would reduce fatal car wrecks </h3><p>While traffic deaths are declining nationwide, Illinois is falling behind. We are one of only 10 states that did <a class="Link" href="https://crashstats.nhtsa.dot.gov/Api/Public/ViewPublication/813800" target="_blank" >not see a decrease last year, </a>and fatalities remain <a class="Link" href="https://www-fars.nhtsa.dot.gov/states/statesfatalitiesfatalityrates.aspx" target="_blank" >more than 15% higher</a> than in 2019.</p><p>These deaths are predictable and preventable, driven largely by reckless behavior such as excessive speeding and impaired driving. Two key pieces of pending legislation, <a class="Link" href="https://legiscan.com/IL/bill/HB4333/2025" target="_blank" >House Bill 4333</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?GAID=18&DocNum=4948&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=166300&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >House Bill 4948</a>, would help get Illinois back on track.</p><p>House Bill 4333 would address impaired driving by lowering the state’s alcohol concentration threshold from 0.08 to 0.05. Research shows impairment begins well before 0.08, and crash risk rises as blood‑alcohol concentration increases. A 0.05 standard would set clearer expectations for drivers, saving lives and preventing millions of dollars in economic losses each year.</p><p>House Bill 4948 would create an Intelligent Speed Assistance Program focused on the small number of high‑risk drivers whose repeated or excessive speeding endangers everyone. The technology limits a vehicle’s speed to stop this dangerous behavior before it causes a tragedy. The program creates an alternative to license suspension for this small population of high-risk drivers.</p><p>These bills target two leading causes of deadly crashes and would help our state go from a lagging outlier to a leader in preventing roadway deaths. We urge the public to ask their legislators to advance these bills.</p><p><i>Nick Jarmusz, director of public affairs, AAA – The Auto Club Group</i></p><p><i>Erin Doherty, regional executive director, Mothers Against Drunk Driving</i></p><p><i>Amy Cohen, founder and president, Families for Safe Streets</i></p><h3>Gun safety </h3><p>Firmly believing the Second Amendment is unambiguous and provides U.S. citizens the right to bear arms, I also agree with Kathleen Sances' <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/other-views/2026/04/15/ban-guns-pistols-converted-illegal-machine-guns-switches-violence-shootings-illinois-kathleen-sances" target="_blank" >recent op-ed</a> in the Sun-Times and believe common sense dictates that some arms are inherently dangerous, and the law should not permit their ownership by civilians.</p><p>Handguns that can be converted into an automatic weapon by the flip of a “switch” can certainly provide personal protection. Still, they also ring up greater body counts. The volume of bullets that can rapidly be discharged by these guns increases the chances of innocents being shot and killed.</p><p>Even those who strongly oppose gun control should support the ban on the sale of weapons that can be converted into machine guns. The Second Amendment can be enforced without throwing common sense out the window.</p><p><i>Terry Takash, Western Springs</i></p><h3>Trump runs country like cartel </h3><p>In this one man’s opinion, I believe our government has been taken over by the "Donald Cartel." Care for U. S. citizens and other countries has become nonexistent. The cartel hides behind all of his own misgivings by using every diversion and lie to cover his own rear end.</p><p>Approximately a third of this country is either brainwashed, prejudiced or just stupid. Which is it? You cannot honestly believe all that falls out of his mouth. How long will this go on? Congress has the power to get rid of him. Problem: Get rid of the head of the cartel, and the lieutenant steps in.</p><p>Is there no end to kissing Trump's rear end? Show some guts. What we're seeing is not my idea of patriotism. I’m afraid to put my flag out because somebody will think I approve.</p><p><i>Tom Konz, U.S. veteran, Portage Park</i></p><h3>Brass knuckles or knucklehead?</h3><p>Donald Trump is making death threats, thumping his chest and rattling his saber over his Iran war and his impotent ability to keep that country from developing nuclear weapons. Does anyone remember this same “very stable genius” tore up the <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/world/fact-checking-trumps-comments-that-a-2015-deal-gave-iran-the-right-to-nuclear-weapons" target="_blank" >international treaty with Iran</a>, negotiated by the Obama administration, that prevented Iran from developing such weapons and subjected them to international inspections? As David Hare said, “No one but a fool is always right."</p><p><i>Jim Pomis, Crystal Lake</i></p><h3>Selective outrage</h3><p>Two members of Congress resign after being accused of sexual attacks. One presidential candidate is elected after being found liable for sexual abuse in a civil case.</p><p><i>Richard Barsanti, Western Springs</i></p>

entries.title: How the war in Iran is affecting Chicago colleges, from canceled research to uncertain post-grad plans
entries.summary: <p>University of Chicago professor Mehrnoush Soroush has been “unfunctional” since late February.</p><p>Soroush, who grew up in Iran and whose work in archaeology focuses on the Middle East, has struggled to communicate with her loved ones since the United States and Israel attacked her home country.</p><p>“My people, my civilians, my family, every part of my country, my cultural heritage, everything is being bombed,” she said.</p><p>For weeks, and now as the end of President Donald Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/07/ceasefire-brings-little-relief-from-psychological-warfare-for-chicago-residents-with-ties-to-iran" target="_blank" ><u>two-week ceasefire draws near</u></a>, she and other Iranians studying or working at universities in the U.S. have struggled to focus on school and work, distracted by the fear their loved ones abroad aren’t safe.</p><p>On college campuses across Illinois, students and faculty are grappling with the effects of the war, which has <a class="Link" href="https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/1/us-israel-attacks-on-iran-death-toll-and-injuries-live-tracker" target="_blank" ><u>killed more than 2,500 people</u></a> in Iran and injured another 26,000 as of Tuesday, according to the news outlet Al Jazeera, relying primarily on official government health and defense sources. Thousands more in <a class="Link" href="https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/how-many-people-have-been-killed-us-israel-war-iran-2026-04-07/" target="_blank" ><u>Lebanon and across the region</u></a> have also been killed or injured.</p><p>In the U.S., some students are not sure if they’ll be able to return home after graduation, while others trying to remain here have not been able to get their visas renewed.</p><p>The war has also had tangible effects on teaching and learning, with some colleges, including the University of Chicago, canceling or postponing study abroad and research programs in the region due to the violence. And the conflict has raised larger questions about free speech on college campuses and the state of anti-war activism, as some students hesitate to protest during the Trump presidency.</p><p>For Soroush, who moved to Tehran for college and got married there, the pain of knowing backdrops to cherished memories are now in ruins has become part of her research.</p><p>She created the <a class="Link" href="https://heritagewatch.camelab.net/" target="_blank" ><u>Middle East Cultural Heritage at Risk in Armed Conflict project</u></a> to document the destruction in Iran. The project has mapped nearly 100 sites so far, and Soroush estimates many more will be added.</p><p>“There is a famous song, and it’s beautifully sang … and [it] says that my home is on fire,” Soroush said. It’s how she envisions Iran now. “I just literally feel like it’s on fire.”</p><h3>How the Iran war is affecting campus climate</h3><p>In recent weeks, some colleges have evacuated students or staff from the Middle East. Northwestern University, for example, relocated “a small number” of graduate students doing research in the area, a spokesperson said.</p><p>Northwestern also increased safety measures at its Qatar campus, after Iran launched a retaliatory strike on a U.S. air base 16 miles away. That has included shelter-in-place orders, remote work and evacuations, <a class="Link" href="https://dailynorthwestern.com/2026/04/07/campus/the-daily-explains-a-timeline-of-how-irans-retaliatory-strikes-have-impacted-nu-q/" target="_blank" ><u>according to the school’s student newspaper</u></a>, The Daily Northwestern.</p><p>Many Iranians’ and Iranian Americans’ views of their government have been muddied by the war. Within the Iranian diaspora, <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/02/world/middleeast/iran-war-diaspora.html" target="_blank" ><u>people’s opinions on the war vary broadly</u></a>, Soroush said. Many opposed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the Supreme Leader of Iran who was killed Feb. 28 in a joint U.S.-Israeli attack, and welcomed his death but condemn the ongoing attacks.</p><p>Mina Rezaei, an Iranian PhD student at Southern Illinois University Carbondale, said she and her husband danced when they heard about the death of Khamenei, who ruled over their home country with near-absolute authority and whose forces reportedly <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/world/more-than-7000-dead-in-irans-crackdown-on-protests-activists-say" target="_blank" ><u>killed more than 7,000 Iranian protesters and activists</u></a> earlier this year.</p><p>“I can say that when Ayatollah Khamenei was dead, I was very happy,” she said. “We are thankful to President Trump, honestly. But now, things are very complicated.”</p><p>They’re grateful Iran isn’t under Khamenei’s rule anymore but worry about the safety of their loved ones back home.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A small group of Iranian-Americans who oppose the current Iranian regime rallied in downtown Chicago on April 7, demanding the United States and Israel not target civilian infrastructure.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The range of views on the war has affected how students and faculty offer one another support.</p><p>In the past, Soroush found solace with other Iranians when violence hit the Middle East. She would invite students to her home, where they typically shared stories of Iran, unpacked current events and leaned on one another.</p><p>But now, she hesitates because she doesn’t know which side they’re on, she said, and she finds it difficult to connect with people who are celebrating the war.</p><p>During past conflicts, "I would just send an email to all the Iranian students and say, ‘Come over. You can feel some comfort in my home,’” Soroush said. “But then some of them will show up and be pro-war, right? And I can't do that because I need to protect myself.”</p><p>The war has shifted things on campuses in other ways, too.</p><p>Omer Mozaffar, the Muslim chaplain and a professor of Islamic studies at Loyola University Chicago, said he’s seen signs in his classes of students grappling with the conflict.</p><p>Students are more withdrawn and distracted, and some have stopped participating in class as much as they typically do, he said. Some are skipping class more often and struggling to get assignments done. He’s heard rumblings of students questioning the possibility of being drafted into the military.</p><p>“It raises big questions about fairness in the world and unfairness in the world,” Mozaffar said. “How does this world operate? Young people are trying to navigate that.”</p><h3>Fewer on-campus protests than in the past</h3><p>College campuses have a history of organized anti-war protests, from mass demonstrations against the Vietnam War in the 1970s to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/reset-with-sasha-ann-simons/2024/05/06/chicagos-pro-palestine-encampments-the-latest-from-uchicago-depaul-saic-and-more" target="_blank" ><u>pro-Palestinian encampments that cropped up on Chicago campuses</u></a> and elsewhere in 2024.</p><p>Students are just as interested in what’s happening in Iran and how it connects to the history of the Middle East and American foreign policy, said Jessica Winegar, a professor of anthropology and Middle East studies at Northwestern.</p><p>“Frankly, a lot of students become very surprised when they learn about the history of U.S. involvement in the region, and often that can make them angry and want to do something about it,” she said.</p><p>It’s not uncommon for Americans to be unaware of the decades of damaging sanctions against Iran or misunderstand the history between the two countries, said one Chicago-based PhD student who grew up in Iran.</p><p>“This emerging collective consciousness among Americans about politics and what is happening in the war, that could open up opportunities around collective organizing and collective action,” said the young woman, who spoke with the Chicago Sun-Times on the condition of anonymity because she fears doxxing and harassment.</p><p>While many students participated in demonstrations aftr the Oct. 7, 2023, attacks on Israel by Hamas and Israel’s subsequent bombardment of Gaza, Loyola’s Mozaffar said the anti-war sentiment is taking a different shape now.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-df0000" name="image-df0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5fe7376/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+166/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5e%2Ff7%2F7af2db3f49dc9667d75f8b733a8c%2Fnucampus-042624-6.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a949f0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+166/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5e%2Ff7%2F7af2db3f49dc9667d75f8b733a8c%2Fnucampus-042624-6.jpg" alt="Signs protesting the war in Gaza were affixed to a fence at Northwestern University in 2024." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a949f0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+166/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5e%2Ff7%2F7af2db3f49dc9667d75f8b733a8c%2Fnucampus-042624-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0b8986d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+166/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5e%2Ff7%2F7af2db3f49dc9667d75f8b733a8c%2Fnucampus-042624-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a949f0d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+166/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5e%2Ff7%2F7af2db3f49dc9667d75f8b733a8c%2Fnucampus-042624-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Pro-Palestinian picket signs were affixed to a fence at Northwestern University in April 2024, as hundreds of students, faculty and others set up an encampment to demand the school divest from Israeli institutions.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Regarding the attacks on Iran, the displeasure is the same as it was regarding the attacks on Oct. 7 and Israel’s response,” he said. “But we barely have any protests taking place on campuses.”</p><p>Students and professors are more cautious after seeing the pro-Palestinian encampments draw counter-protesters and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2024/05/31/by-the-numbers-how-long-did-the-pro-palestinian-student-encampments-in-chicago-last" target="_blank" ><u>be cleared out by police</u></a>, Mozaffar said. And since Trump has returned to office and <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/show/how-trumps-college-crackdown-is-raising-concerns-about-free-speech-and-academic-freedom" target="_blank" ><u>taken steps to limit certain kinds of speech on college campuses</u></a>, the fear has intensified, Mozaffar said.</p><p>Mozaffar said fears of doxxing and harassment have quieted some students who otherwise may have been more vocal about their opposition to the U.S. and Israel’s attacks on Iran. He has similar fears that speaking about the war in class brings some risk of harassment or criticism from Trump and other right-wing politicians.</p><p>But Mozaffar continues to discuss the war in class, encouraging students to debate and raise counterpoints, including to their own points of view.</p><p>“For the sake of our society, even with those fears, we still have to discuss these things,” he said.</p><p>The comparative lack of acknowledgement of the Iran war has disappointed some students and faculty members with ties to the country. Soroush, for example, said the University of Chicago hasn’t sent a school-wide email acknowledging the war and its consequences, but the school did so after Russia invaded Ukraine.</p><p>“We understand the pain and difficulty this brings – for members of the University community from Ukraine, Russia and Europe, and others who hoped a major conflict could be avoided,” University of Chicago officials wrote in a 2022 email obtained by the Sun-Times.</p><p>Soroush had hoped to read something similar when the war in Iran began.</p><p>“We’re not asking you to take a side,” she said. “We’re asking you to express solidarity, empathy, acknowledge the fact that there are impacted communities.”</p><p>A University of Chicago spokesperson didn’t respond to questions about university communications regarding the war.</p><h3>War complicates some students’ post-graduation plans</h3><p>The war has forced some students to rethink their post-college plans.</p><p>Rezaei’s husband, Kaveh Javanshirjavid, graduated with his PhD from Southern Illinois University Carbondale in December. It was the same month the <a class="Link" href="https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/12/restricting-and-limiting-the-entry-of-foreign-nationals-to-protect-the-security-of-the-united-states/" target="_blank" ><u>Trump administration stopped issuing visas</u></a> to people from certain countries, including Iran. Now Javanshirjavid can’t get a work visa, and Rezaei expects to have the same problem when she graduates later this year.</p><p>The family has been living on Rezaei’s small research assistant salary for the last four months. Javanshirjavid had a job lined up in the soybean industry, and Rezaei has a faculty job in statistics she hopes to start in July. But now those career plans are on hold.</p><p>Typically, they would ask family members in Iran for help. But they don’t want to do that with their families facing danger.</p><p>“We used our savings, but now I don't know what we want to do,” Rezaei said. “It’s very stressful."</p><p>The Chicago-based PhD student is also reconsidering her post-grad plans. Until recently, she had planned to return to Iran after she completes her degree. Her family and friends are still in Iran, and she keeps in touch with local activist networks there. But now, she worries that in a few years it will no longer be possible to live there.</p><p>“I don’t know if the country is the same country that I once left,” she said. “[But] I still have this faith that Iranian people can again rise from the ashes of the country.”</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Mystery shrouds ex-Mormon figure's death in northern Illinois plane crash amid child sexual abuse lawsuit
entries.summary: <p>Richard McClung died in a plane crash Feb. 14 when the small aircraft he was piloting plummeted into a residential neighborhood between McHenry County and Rockford, narrowly missing homes, severing a gas line and horrifying locals.</p><p>McClung’s online death notice described the 83-year-old Rockford resident as having “a love for his family” who “tried his best in all the ways he knew how.”</p><p>A darker reality has since emerged: McClung was a former Mormon church figure in Illinois and registered sex offender who’s been repeatedly accused of sexually abusing children.</p><p>At the time of his death, McClung was a defendant in a federal lawsuit in Chicago accusing him of molesting a young congregant while he was a member and leader of a Mormon church in Rockford in the early 2000s. </p><p>The death notice said the crash was “a result of having a mechanical failure.”</p><p>A nonprofit general aviation safety organization says McClung likely plunged his airplane into the ground because of mounting pressure from recent court filings.</p><p>Lawyers involved in the suit wouldn’t comment.</p><p>This much is known: A formal cause of death hasn’t yet been determined by the Boone County coroner, and the National Transportation Safety Board also hasn’t concluded its investigation. An arm of the federal government, the NTSB is generally the final word on how and why a fatal plane crash happened.</p><p>Filed in 2025, the lawsuit also says certain Illinois leaders of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints knew about at least one sex abuse allegation against McClung and still let him serve at the Rockford congregation where he had access to children, including the accuser now suing.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7c0000" name="image-7c0000"></a> class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>NTSB</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Salt Lake City-based denomination is a defendant in the case — which remains pending — and has denied wrongdoing.</p><p>While the Latter-day Saints, or LDS, has been rocked with a child sex abuse scandal in the West where most adherents live, more accusations have begun surfacing in Illinois.</p><p>Just recently, the Sun-Times reported <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/05/mormon-church-child-sex-abuse-cover-up-crystal-lake-latter-day-saints-congregation-wade-christofferson" target="_blank" >Wade Christofferson</a> — a brother of Mormon apostle <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/30/mormon-apostle-d-todd-christofferson-latter-day-saints-wade-christofferson-child-sexual-abuse-church" target="_blank" >D. Todd Christofferson</a> — is suspected of molesting multiple children, including while he was a member and leader of a Latter-day Saints congregation in far northwest suburban Crystal Lake in the 1980s and 1990s.</p><p>D. Todd Christofferson is part of the church’s powerful “first presidency” headed by former Chicago lawyer Dallin H. Oaks.</p><p>The lawsuit doesn’t name the Mormon leaders who allegedly let McClung stay active in Rockford despite knowing about one or more prior abuse allegations. But a deposition recently given for the case by the mother of another McClung accuser claims Oaks’ son-in-law, Jack Ward, is one of them.</p><p>A Blue Island native, Ward was president of the Latter-day Saints’ Chicago Temple in Glenview until last year, according to church web sites.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-720000" name="image-720000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fa3887c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/19c0ef3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7842ffd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jack Ward, at left, and his father-in-law, Mormon leader Dallin H. Oaks." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7842ffd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8cece0a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7842ffd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/992x557+0+36/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2Ff0%2Facd7ed8749b4a3bdb3170e283d64%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-16-at-1-15-46-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jack Ward, at left, and his father-in-law, Mormon leader Dallin H. Oaks.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, YouTube</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>He couldn’t be reached for comment, but court records show he’s being called for a deposition in the case.</p><p>The pending federal lawsuit says at the time McClung “abused plaintiff, he had been charged with the sexual abuse of another minor girl in September 2006, at which time church was put on notice of the danger that perpetrator posed to plaintiff and other minor children.</p><p>“Yet the LDS church did nothing to protect the young children of their community, in continuing to allow perpetrator to be on LDS church property, and maintain his leadership position within the LDS church, as Second Counselor to the Bishop.”</p><p>Congregants weren’t told, so parents and children didn’t know McClung was a danger — and McClung molested two girls during this time: the accuser in the suit and her friend, whose mother recently gave the deposition, court records allege.</p><p>“LDS church ratified perpetrator’s sexual abuse and sexual assault of plaintiff because LDS church had knowledge that perpetrator had class="Figure-credit"><p>Robert Herguth / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>McClung was convicted in 2014 on some of the 2011 sex offenses and sentenced to prison.</p><p>Around 4 p.m. on Feb. 14, McClung’s “experimental, amateur-built” single-engine Tailwind plane hit the ground between two homes in the Poplar Grove community, destroying a fence.</p><p>He was the sole occupant. A small airport was nearby.</p><p>“According to witnesses, the pilot attended a local fly-in event at Cottonwood airport ... and was returning to Poplar Grove Airport ... at the time of the accident,” the NTSB says in a preliminary report. “The pilot departed [a runway] about 15 minutes before the accident. The pilot was not in communication with air traffic control” during the flight.</p><p>Upon impact, the “engine was embedded about 3 feet into the ground. The propeller hub and remnants of the wooden propeller were impact separated and buried beneath the engine.”</p><p>A neighbor said a group of children was playing outside not far away at the time. A natural gas line was broken, spurring a temporary evacuation of the area.</p><p>The NTSB report also said: “A witness who was working in his driveway when he heard the airplane stated that it sounded louder than normal. When he looked up, the airplane was in a nosedive and then impacted the ground in his yard.”</p><p>A woman who lives at the house with the wrecked fence said she was at home at the time of the impact and has no idea how or why the plane ended up on her property — she didn’t know McClung and isn’t a member of his church.</p>
entries.author: Robert Herguth

entries.title: Des Plaines River cresting, but flooding along Fox expected to worsen
entries.summary: <p>Residents in communities along the Des Plaines River should see floodwaters begin to recede Monday, but their counterparts living beside the Fox River may not be as fortunate.</p><p>Both rivers swelled following the latest round of heavy storms Friday night, with the Des Plaines River expected to reach major flood stage at some point Monday in <a class="Link" href="https://water.noaa.gov/gauges/ldri2" target="_blank" >Lincolnshire </a>and just below it in <a class="Link" href="https://water.noaa.gov/gauges/guni2" target="_blank" >Gurnee</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://water.noaa.gov/gauges/dmpi2" target="_blank" >Des Plaines</a>, according to the National Water Prediction Service.</p><p>Forecasters predict the Fox River to continue rising in coming days, cresting just below 12 feet — considered major flood stage — Thursday in Algonquin.</p><p>According to a flood warning issued Sunday, at 12 feet structures would be threatened along River Street, Johnson Street and Maiden Lane in East Dundee; North Harrison Street in Algonquin; and Washington Street in Carpentersville. Bayview Road and low-lying areas of Festival Park in Elgin also would be at risk, according to the alert.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260419/news/des-plaines-river-cresting-but-flooding-along-fox-expected-to-worsen/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.</p>
entries.author: Steve Zalusky l Daily Herald

entries.title: Transformer blows at Kluczynski building in Loop: CFD
entries.summary: <p>No one was injured when a ComEd transformer blew on the 40th floor of the Kluczynski Federal Building in the Loop Monday morning, a spokesperson with the fire department confirmed. </p><p>The situation was under control, though some elevators were not working, as of about 8:10 a.m. at the high-rise building, located at 230 S. Dearborn St., fire officials said. </p><p>There were people in the building, though none of them were injured, according to the fire department. </p><p>ComEd crews are on the scene, repairing the transformer, according to a spokesperson. </p><p>No further information was immediately available. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-o/" target="_blank" >Casey Toner </a>and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-b/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Fatal shooting</b>: After Officer Carlos Baker fatally shot his partner, Krystal Rivera, the Chicago cop told investigators he would "die for her." But newly released body camera footage shows Baker ran for cover after he fired the deadly gunshot and took more than 90 seconds before checking on Rivera as she was gasping for breath.</p><p><b>Footage released</b>: The footage, released Friday by the city’s Civilian Office of Police Accountability, follows Baker and Rivera as they chase an armed man into a Chatham apartment on June 5, 2025. After Baker kicks down the door, they’re met by a second man who aims a rifle at them. That’s when Baker turns and appears to stumble before he shoots Rivera and runs up a stairwell. After calling for backup and reporting that he and Rivera were fired upon, Baker tends to Rivera and carries her downstairs, where other officers are waiting.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: During an interview with COPA, Baker told investigators he was "best friends" with Rivera and that he "would never" intentionally shoot her. But Rivera’s mother filed a wrongful death lawsuit last year, alleging Baker had been struggling to accept her daughter’s decision to end their romantic relationship when he fatally shot her during amid banner first year</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-tj/" target="_blank" >Ximena N. Beltran Quan Kiu</a></h5><p>Just days before Afro-diaspora fusion restaurant Mahari celebrated its anniversary in January, it was named Best Neighborhood Restaurant at the 2026 Banchet Awards, beating out more established operators.</p><p>Mahari executive chef and owner Rahim Muhammad accepted the prize, shouting out Hyde Park, his birthplace, and adding, "We’re not from a large family or money … we’re just from the Chicago streets and we bring elegance from where we’re from."</p><p>Magic Johnson, Muhammad’s business partner and the eatery's director of community and culture, echoes the sentiment.</p><p>"I’ve been in Hyde Park since 2011 and the bond that I have with this community ... is something special," Johnson said.</p><p>Beyond its local roots, Mahari's team also wants to emphasize its diasporic connections — boasting a menu that's an ambitious medley of flavors, dishes and ingredients that crosses and crisscrosses borders, oceans and cultures.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-tt/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>On Friday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-td/" target="_blank" ><b>What’s the first vinyl album you ever bought, and from where?</b> </a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"'I Am Woman' by Helen Reddy, from Little’s Music in Oak Park. For a 10-year-old, $3.98 was a big chunk of birthday money." — <i>Marty Regan</i></p><p>"My first album was 'Tapestry' by Carole King, purchased from the local record store near North and Central Avenues in Chicago. I was so proud to buy it with my own money." — <i>Christine Bock</i></p><p>"The first album I recall purchasing was Jimmy Smith's 'The Cat,' in the mid-60s. Jazz organ and his visits to the Plugged Nickel in Old Town drove my desire to dive deeper into jazz." — <i>Richard Bell</i> </p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wihiuhd-l-th/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>

entries.title: Judge tosses lawsuit over Trump's push to deploy National Guard to Chicago after his Supreme Court loss
entries.summary: <p>Six months after she blocked President Donald Trump’s efforts <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/09/federal-judge-set-to-hear-arguments-in-high-stakes-national-guard-hearing" target="_blank" >to deploy National Guard troops</a> into Chicago, a federal judge Monday confirmed that the orders triggering the deployment<b> </b>are no longer in effect<b> </b>and dismissed the lawsuit that challenged them.</p><p>U.S. District Judge April Perry ruled that the case is “moot,”<b> </b>meaning there’s no meaningful action left for her to take. But she warned that there are still “fail safes”<b> </b>built into the legal system, especially if it turns out there were “misrepresentations”<b> </b>from the Trump administration.</p><p>“The court cannot provide ongoing protection against hypothetical unlawful acts committed by the federal government,” Perry said.</p><p>The judge’s ruling comes despite a Dec. 31 social media post by Trump, commenting on his administration’s plans for Chicago, in which he promised “we will come back, perhaps in a much different and stronger form, when crime begins to soar again — Only a question of time!”</p><p>Perry told the courtroom, “I honestly don’t know what to make of those particular social media messages.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" >U.S. Supreme Court sides against Trump, won’t lift block on National Guard deployment in Illinois</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Given that the president’s comments were “about general crime control, which is not at all what this deployment was supposed to be about,” Perry said the president’s commentary was “not helpful enough” to find any exception in the law that would allow her to keep the case alive.</p><p>Attorney General Kwame<b> </b>Raoul<b> </b>later reacted to the ruling by saying American people “should not live under threat of military occupation simply because they live in a jurisdiction that has fallen out of a president's political favor.”</p><p>“I am pleased that today, the court has declared the Trump administration’s unlawful orders defunct and said it is absolutely clear that the administration cannot use the Illinois orders to federalize or deploy National Guard troops in Illinois,” Raoul said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-510000" name="image-510000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2806447/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/586ffa9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad1b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg" alt="US-HEALTH-VIRUS-SUPREME COURT" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad1b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0f90ac0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ad1b5dc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5222x2931+0+204/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2Fbd%2F798996b4ee7187aca9b332885e3f%2F1211716552-jpg.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The US Supreme Court is seen in Washington, DC, on May 4, 2020.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo by SAUL LOEB/AFP via Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Perry’s ruling brings to an end a historic lawsuit filed in the heat of the aggressive deportation campaign known as Operation Midway Blitz. The case eventually led to a rare defeat for the Trump administration <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" target="_blank" >before the U.S. Supreme Court</a>.</p><p>That’s despite a White House spokesperson’s claim that the president “exercised his lawful authority to protect federal officers and assets”<b> </b>amid<b> </b>“ongoing violent riots and lawlessness.”</p><p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have yet to convict anyone of violence directed toward federal agents during Midway Blitz. One man <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/15/crime-chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-ice-border-patrol" target="_blank" >pleaded guilty</a> last week to concealing information about a crime, and in doing so he admitted he drove his Ford F-250<b> </b>into the rear passenger side of a federal vehicle.</p><p>The events in that case took place one day before Trump triggered deployment of the National Guard into Chicago.</p><p>Perry ruled Oct. 9 that the Trump administration’s “perception of events”<b> </b>around Chicago were “simply unreliable.” She found “no credible evidence that there is danger of rebellion in the state of Illinois,” nor that Trump was “unable … to execute the laws of the United States.”</p><p>The judge blocked deployment, and the Supreme Court eventually sided against Trump, as well.</p><p>Perry on Monday focused on whether deployment orders from Defense Secretary Pete<b> </b>Hegseth<b> </b>remained in effect. Justice Department lawyer Stephen<b> </b>Tagert told the judge they’d effectively been undone through an oral order by Hegseth on Dec. 31.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/09/federal-judge-set-to-hear-arguments-in-high-stakes-national-guard-hearing" >Federal judge hits Trump administration credibility in siding with city and state against National Guard deployment</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Much of Monday’s hearing revolved around whether Perry could view a record that documented Hegseth’s Dec. 31 order. Tagert said he was not authorized to immediately make it public, but might be able to obtain that clearance eventually. However, an attorney for the state of Illinois said he’d already obtained a copy through Gov. JB Pritzker’s office.</p><p>The lawyers eventually met with Perry in chambers. Then, she publicly read portions of the record documenting Hegseth’s order. Bottom line, she said, Hegseth’s oral order “killed”<b> </b>a previous extension of troop deployment<b> </b>through the middle of this month.</p><p>That, combined with the Supreme Court’s ruling in December, all meant that it “cannot reasonably be expected”<b> </b>that the Trump administration would try again to deploy troops under the orders issued last October. And she said she can’t rule on hypothetical scenarios.</p><p>“Things in Chicago are calm,”<b> </b>Perry said. “They have been calm for many, many months.”</p>
