entries.title: Chicago gang boss Luther 'Bro Man' Spann says he had 'nothing to do with the Four Corner Hustlers' as he gets life term
entries.summary: <p>Federal authorities say <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2021/10/26/22747190/labar-spann-racketeering-trial-accused-four-corner-hustlers-chief-takes-stand-prosecutors-rest-case" >Labar<b> </b>“Bro Man”<b> </b>Spann</a> “inflicted widespread terror on the West Side of Chicago,” murdered people “indiscriminately”<b> </b>and bragged about the deaths of his victims.</p><p>But Monday, nearly five years after Spann’s original conviction, U.S. District Judge Thomas<b> </b>Durkin<b> </b>finally handed down a life sentence to the onetime chief of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/9/17/18458884/four-corner-hustlers-boss-labar-spann-to-judge-this-is-my-life-on-the-line" >Four Corner Hustlers</a> street gang.</p><p>“You’ll breathe your last breath in jail,” Durkin told Spann. “You’ll die in jail. That won’t bring back the people you killed.”</p><p>The judge also told Spann that it brought him “no pleasure”<b> </b>to hand down the sentence — prompting a laugh from Spann in the courtroom. Spann insisted during the hearing he had “nothing to do with the Four Corner Hustlers.”</p><p>“I’m my own man,” he said.</p><p>Durkin had no choice<b> </b>but to give Spann life after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/12/15/four-corner-hustlers-chief-facing-life-in-prison-after-jurors-hand-him-second-conviction-in-four-years" target="_blank" >a jury convicted the gang chief</a> in December of racketeering conspiracy and other crimes, holding him responsible for four murders.</p><p>But it’s not the first time Spann faced sentencing. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2021/11/8/22770948/federal-jury-convicts-four-corner-hustlers-chief-labar-bro-man-spann" target="_blank" >An earlier conviction</a> in 2021 was undone by revelations<b> </b>that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/04/24/four-corner-hustler-chiefs-conviction-threatened-feds" target="_blank" >a former prosecutor made an “unauthorized”<b> </b>promise</a> to a key witness. That led to Spann’s second trial, which took place over six weeks last fall.</p><p>Prosecutors<b> </b>told jurors that Spann and his gang “terrorized”<b> </b>the West Side for decades. Though he’d been paralyzed for much of his life as a result of a 1999 shooting, Assistant U.S. Attorney Emily<b> </b>Vermylen<b> </b>warned jurors not to be fooled.</p><p>“Make no mistake,” she said during opening statements<b> </b>in the trial last fall, “the defendant sitting in front of you is a killer.”</p><p>Prosecutors outlined the need for a life sentence in a recent court memo, noting that Spann’s co-conspirators “ruthlessly murdered, extorted, and robbed anyone in their way, from rival gang members, to law enforcement cooperators, to innocent bystanders.”</p><p>Spann’s attorneys argued for a lighter sentence on certain counts while acknowledging the life sentence Durkin would hand down on others. They filed their own memo outlining Spann’s difficult childhood, in which he grew up in neighborhoods “plagued with severe crime.”</p><p>They told the judge Spann could not read when the feds locked him up, but he’s since taught himself to read, including<b> </b>“long excerpts of case law.”</p><p>Spann is tied to the killings of Maximillion<b> </b>McDaniel<b> </b>in July 2000; of George King in April 2003; of Willie Woods also in April 2003; and of Latin Kings chief Rudy “Kato” Rangel in June 2003.</p><p>The feds wrote that, after McDaniel was shot in the back of the head, Spann visited McDaniel’s brother in jail.<b> </b>The brother later testified that, during the visit, Spann told him,<b> </b>“don’t end up like your brother … who you think kill[ed] Max? … We killed Max.”</p><p>Two<b> </b>fellow Four Corner Hustlers killed Woods in 2003 on Spann’s orders, walking up to him and shooting him in the face, prosecutors wrote. Woods’ girlfriend described at trial how it happened right in front of her, and how she unsuccessfully tried to drag Woods into a car to get help.</p><p>The same men shot King several times, including once in the back of his head as he lay on the ground, while Spann was in the midst of a dispute with a rival faction of their gang, according to the feds.</p><p>Spann was offered $20,000 for Rangel’s death, and he sent someone to shoot Rangel “at point-blank range” while Rangel was getting a haircut, prosecutors wrote.</p><p>“The harm caused by [Spann] and his co-conspirators is real and permanent,” they argued. “His actions ended the lives of four men, depriving their families of time with their loved ones. The harm and pain caused to these families can never be repaired.</p><p>“A life sentence for these murders is the only sentence that properly serves the sentencing purpose of retribution.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/public-safety/2025/12/15/four-corner-hustlers-chief-facing-life-in-prison-after-jurors-hand-him-second-conviction-in-four-years" target="_blank" >Four Corner Hustlers chief facing life in prison after jurors hand him second conviction in four years</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-gangs/2025/11/10/labar-spann-four-corner-hustlers-retrial" target="_blank" >‘The defendant … is a killer': Second trial begins for accused Four Corner Hustlers chief Labar Spann</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/10/13/18388634/reputed-gang-boss-labar-spann-s-cook-county-jail-reign-of-terror" target="_blank" >Reputed gang boss Labar Spann’s Cook County Jail reign of terror</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2018/7/20/18371447/killing-kato-rudy-rangel-latin-kings-four-corner-hustlers" >Killing ‘Kato’: the story of Latin Kings boss Rudy Rangel Jr.’s murder</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Six dogs killed, found in garbage bags near Crete
entries.summary: <p>Will County State's Attorney James Glasgow momentarily became emotional while speaking about the discovery of six dogs found dead in garbage bags in a ditch last month in unincorporated Crete.</p><p>"Anyone who could do this to six living things is an incredibly dangerous person," Glasgow said at a press conference Monday morning in Joliet. "I don't want to diminish crimes against people for one second, but this is something out of a really bad movie."</p><p>The pitbull-like dogs, all between nine months to a-year-and-a-half old, had bite marks that showed signs of dog fighting and were found by Crete Township highway maintenance officials near Norfolk Avenue and Kings Road on March 3, officials said.</p><p>Information on how the dogs were killed wasn’t immediately available. </p><p>Jay Bergman, Chairman of First Secure Bank, offered a $5,000 reward to be given to any tipster who gives information leading to an arrest and charges.</p><p>"If you know something about this terrible crime, provide a tip to the sheriff's department and within several weeks, that person can be $5,000 richer," Bergman said. "More importantly, we'll get this sicko off the street before he harms a person or animal."</p><p>The Will County sheriff's office were conducting an active investigation and stressed the importance of making an arrest in the case.</p><p>"This was a gruesome and horrifying discovery," said Jeremy Zdzinicki, deputy chief at the sheriff's office. "We need help from the general public to get justice for these animals. They didn't deserve to die this way."</p><p>Officials stressed that the gruesome crime acted as a broader public safety issue without if left unsolved. </p><p>"Research shows that people that engage in this level of animal cruelty often involved with high level illegal activities, including violence against people," Anna Payton, administrator with the Will County Animal Protection Services, said. "We cannot keep people or animals in the community safe without your help." </p><p>"This is not just an animal cruelty case, this is a public safety issue," Glasgow said in an earlier statement. "There is zero tolerance for this kind of cruelty in Will County — and we will not stop until there is justice."</p><p>Anyone with information is encouraged to reach out to the Will County Crime Stoppers by calling 800-323-6734, texting 274367 or visiting willcountycrimestoppers.org. Tipsters can remain anonymous.</p><p>"These dogs deserve better, our community deserves better," Payton said. "Together, we can have something good come out of this horrific event. If you see something, say something."</p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Bears TE Cole Kmet back for seventh season, says, 'Never really felt I would be playing anywhere else'
entries.summary: <p>The moment <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >the Bears</a> surprisingly drafted tight end Colston Loveland with the No. 10 pick a year ago, mainstay Cole Kmet’s future with the team got cloudy. While new coach Ben Johnson planned to use two tight ends, Kmet’s big salary raised questions about how much longer he’d be with the team.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/08/04/last-stand-bears-te-cole-kmet-is-once-again-forced-to-convince-a-new-coach-he-can-be-a-difference-maker" >Kmet openly wondered about that last season</a>, but even after one of his least productive seasons as a pass catcher, he trusted that Johnson and general manager Ryan Poles valued his subtle contributions to the NFL’s No. 6 offense.</p><p>“I did a lot of the things they asked me to do,” Kmet said Monday at the start of the Bears’ offseason training program. “Got to my exit meeting with them about right after the game against L.A., and if you were to ask me right after that, I didn’t think I was going anywhere. Kind of felt that throughout the offseason.</p><p>“I also understand that if an offer comes across their plate for them to make a move or them to make a change, they would definitely do that, [but] I never really felt I would be playing anywhere else this year.”</p><p>Kmet, 27, is going into his seventh season with the Bears and is one of three holdovers from general manager Ryan Pace’s tenure along with kicker Cairo Santos and cornerback Jaylon Johnson. Poles gave him a four-year, $50 million contract extension that began in 2024, but it has affordable outs going into this season and next.</p><p>The Bears are set to pay Kmet $11.6 million each of the next two seasons, but can cut him with a $4.2 million dead-salary-cap hit this year or a $1.6 million hit after the season.</p><p>They were in a cap crunch this offseason, but prioritized keeping Kmet and chose to trade wide receiver DJ Moore to the Bills and release linebacker Tremaine Edmunds.</p><p>That decision came despite Loveland showing huge promise last season and Kmet finishing with just 30 catches for 347 yards and two touchdowns — his second-lowest total in each category.</p><p>“The two tight ends are paramount,” Kmet said. “It might not always be in the passing game. There were a lot of things I had to do in pass [blocking] and the run game that proved to be very valuable for the team. I saw that, and more importantly, Ben and Ryan really recognized that as well.”</p><p>Two years earlier, Kmet had a career year with 73 catches for 719 yards and six touchdowns.</p><p>Much like Moore, the dip in production last season wasn’t entirely on him.</p><p>Quarterback Caleb Williams was up and down throughout his first season playing under Johnson and finished 22nd in passer rating (90.1) and last in completion percentage (58.1), and Johnson emphasized establishing a diverse array of skill players to make the Bears less predictable.</p><p>Loveland led the team with 58 catches, which ranked 51st in the league, and Moore was next with 50.</p><p>Kmet could be important to Johnson in several aspects this season, which would justify keeping his salary on the books.</p><p>Johnson often has highlighted the value of being able to line Kmet up inside or outside and his savvy as a blocker. The Bears’ key skill also are very inexperienced, with Loveland, Rome Odunze, Luther Burden and Kyle Monangai all going into their second or third season. Having someone as reliable as Kmet to lead them could make a big difference.</p>
entries.author: Jason Lieser

entries.title: Bulls have started interview process with candidates for new VP role
entries.summary: <p>Michael Reinsdorf admittedly wasn’t working with the widest of nets back in March of 2020.</p><p>There was a global pandemic to deal with, the team president didn’t have the longest list of names in his contacts, and the interview process was old school, done on phone and Zoom calls because of what was going on in the world.</p><p>Those were the circumstances former executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas was hired under.</p><p>“Weird” was how Reinsdorf described it earlier this month.</p><p>Things have changed.</p><p>The Sun-Times confirmed a Monday report that the Bulls and the hiring firm they have been working with have started to receive permission to begin interviewing candidates to replace the recently fired Karnisovas, with a list that included Timberwolves GM Matt Lloyd, Pistons senior VP Dennis Lindsey, Hawks senior VP Bryson Graham, Cavaliers GM Mike Gansey, Spurs assistant GM Dave Telep and basketball agent Austin Brown.</p><p>A source also indicated that there were at least three other mystery candidates involved in the interview process as well.</p><p>“We’ll talk to more people and get a better understanding of what some other organizations do, what things do they do right, what things do they do wrong? How can we get better?” Reinsdorf said of the process when he met with the media several weeks ago.</p><p>He also made it very clear that the time was right for a change and the Bulls were sitting in a very positive direction heading into this offseason.</p><p>“We have all our draft picks going forward,” Reinsdorf said. “We have (injured rookie) Noa (Essengue) coming back, which is basically having a second top 10 pick, and then we have $65 million in cap space. I just feel like now is the time. We’re in position to get this right, clean slate.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Cinco de Mayo parade in Little Village canceled for second year as immigration enforcement concerns persist
entries.summary: <p>For the second year in a row, the annual Cinco de Mayo parade in Little Village has been canceled due to immigration enforcement concerns, organizers said.</p><p>The parade typically draws hundreds to the neighborhood and celebrates Mexico's victory over Napoleon III’s French army in the Battle of Puebla in 1862. But this year, Chicago's Mexican community is still on edge in the wake of "Operation Midway Blitz," the aggressive deportation campaign the Trump administration launched across the Chicago area last fall.</p><p>"Many families are experiencing fear and uncertainty due to increased immigration enforcement actions and the ongoing threat of raids," organizers with the Cermak Road Chamber of Commerce and Casa Puebla said in a statement.</p><p>People are still avoiding public gatherings out of fear, organizers said, adding, "there is nothing to celebrate."</p><p>The event was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/04/10/cinco-de-mayo-parade-canceled-immigration-chicago" >canceled last year,</a> too, for similar reasons. </p><p>"It was a difficult decision to make, but the safety of our community is more important,” organizer Hector Escobar told the Sun-Times last year.</p><p>Since then, Chicago and the suburbs were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/08/trump-chicago-crime-pritzker-national-guard-ice-truth-social" >targeted by masked federal agents</a>, who detained residents while at times deploying chemical irritants like tear gas. Widespread protests and grassroots efforts to protect immigrants followed. The ramped-up enforcement actions eventually waned, but the impact to Latino communities like Little Village, Pilsen and the Southeast Side has lingered.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/20/operation-midway-blitz-effects-latino-neighborhoods-pilsen-little-village-southeast-side" >Operation Midway Blitz fallout still being felt in Chicago’s Latino neighborhoods</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Ford City Mall está a punto de cerrar tras falta de reparaciones
entries.summary: <p>Lo que alguna vez fue un popular destino de compras en el lado suroeste parece estar a punto de cerrar.</p><p>El centro comercial Ford City Mall, que abrió en 1965, podría verse obligado a cerrar después de que su propietario, Namdar Realty Group, no lograra realizar las reparaciones necesarias en el edificio que está en deterioro, incluyendo arreglar un sistema de supresión de incendios que no funciona y evitar inundaciones constantes.</p><p>El juez de la Corte del Condado de Cook, Leonard Murray, dio a los inquilinos siete días para presentar un plan para cerrar sus negocios o desocupar el edificio, tras un cierre ordenado por la corte durante una audiencia el jueves. La próxima audiencia está programada para el 23 de abril.</p><p>“Esto se trata de que Namdar no asume la responsabilidad”, dijo el concejal Derrick Curtis (18°), cuyo distrito incluye el Ford City Mall. “La Municipalidad les ha dicho que se pongan en cumplimiento durante años. Se ha convertido en un problema de seguridad pública”.</p><p>Namdar Realty Group y el Departamento Legal de la Municipalidad no respondieron a una solicitud de comentarios.</p><p>La Municipalidad presentó una moción de emergencia el 10 de abril, meses después de que la compañía de bienes raíces Kurv Industrial, anteriormente Bridge Industrial, revelara <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/07/17/ford-city-mall-ha-ido-perdiendo-su-atractivo-y-podria-ser-convertido-en-bodegas" target="_blank" >planes para demoler el centro comercial</a> ubicado en 7601 S. Cicero Ave. y reconstruir el sitio en un campus industrial de $150 millones.</p><p>La propiedad de casi 60 acres sería despejada para dar paso a cuatro edificios designados para almacenamiento o a la manufactura ligera, aunque no se han proporcionado actualizaciones recientes sobre la propuesta.</p><p>Curtis dijo que Namdar tuvo la oportunidad de revitalizar la propiedad y crear un espacio donde la comunidad pudiera comprar y reunirse. En cambio, dijo, la empresa ha construido una reputación por comprar propiedades en dificultades y “sacar todo”.</p><p>“No creo que sea justo”, dijo Curtis.</p><p>Aparte de los problemas dentro del edificio, el centro comercial, en su mayoría desierto, ha sido una atracción para que los conductores derrapen sus autos (“drift”) en el estacionamiento. Curtis dijo que Namdar se ha negado a contratar seguridad para mantenerlos alejados.</p><p>“Los derrapadores vienen y derrapan en el estacionamiento, hacen vueltas y queman llantas”, dijo. “Realmente ha sido una mala situación con esta empresa en particular”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" $16.6 millones, una gran diferencia comparada a transacciones anteriores. El magnate de bienes raíces <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/5/18/23728642/sam-zell-dies-obituary-billionaire-chicago-equity-group" target="_blank" >Sam Zell</a> tuvo la propiedad durante 25 años, pagando un reportado $75 millones en 1987. Zell se la entregó a su prestamista en 2012.</p><p>Durante la audiencia del jueves, Murray negó una solicitud de un representante de JCPenney para tener 30 días para responder a la moción. JCPenney es la única tienda departamental en el centro comercial.</p><p>Curtis dijo que los inquilinos están preocupados por lo que sucederá con sus contratos de arrendamiento si el juez decide cerrar el edificio. Entre los inquilinos del edificio se encuentran AMC Theatres, Foot Locker y Marshalls.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por</i> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Video muestra que oficial de policía de Chicago tomó refugio por 90 segundos tras dispararle a Krystal Rivera
entries.summary: <p>Después de que el oficial Carlos Baker disparó fatalmente a su compañera Krystal Rivera, el policía de Chicago le dijo a los investigadores que “moriría por ella”.</p><p>Sin embargo, las imágenes recientemente publicadas de la cámara corporal muestran que Baker corrió a cubrirse después de disparar el tiro mortal y le tomó más de 90 segundos antes de verificar cómo se encontraba Rivera mientras ella luchaba por respirar.</p><p>Las imágenes, publicadas el viernes por la Oficina Civil de Responsabilidad Policial (COPA), siguen a Baker y Rivera mientras persiguen a un hombre armado dentro de un apartamento en el vecindario de Chatham el 5 de junio de 2025. Después de que Baker rompe la puerta, se encuentran con un segundo hombre que les apunta con un rifle.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3f0000" name="module-3f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Fue entonces cuando Baker se dio la vuelta y parece tropezar antes de dispararle a Rivera y subir por una escalera. Después de pedir refuerzos e informar inicialmente que él y Rivera fueron atacados a tiros, Baker atiende a Rivera y la lleva escaleras abajo, donde otros oficiales están esperando.</p><p>Durante una entrevista con COPA, Baker les dijo a los investigadores que él y Rivera eran “mejores amigos” y que “nunca” le dispararía intencionalmente. Dijo que se dio cuenta de que era el tirador solo cuando revisó su arma en la sede de detectives del Área 2 y notó que faltaba una bala.</p><p>“Estaba perdido y confundido. Estaba en negación de que incluso disparé mi arma”, le dijo a los investigadores, según grabaciones de entrevistas obtenidas por el Illinois Answers Proyect y el Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Baker dijo que estaba dispuesto a arriesgar su vida para ayudar a Rivera y recordó pensar, “Moriré por ella, que tenía que llegar a ella”.</p><p>Pero la familia de Rivera ha dicho que Baker “la dejó morir”.</p><h3><b>Una relación romántica </b></h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-93aef5" A Rivera le disparó y asesinó su pareja policial, Carlos Baker, durante una persecución a pie en junio de 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>‘Pensé que iba a morir’ </b></h3><p>John Catanzara, presidente del sindicato de la Orden Fraternal de Policía (FOP), defendió la conducta de Baker después de que se publicara el video de la cámara corporal.</p><p>“Muchos oficiales habrían bajado corriendo por las... escaleras, posiblemente de inmediato ... sometiéndose a ser baleados”, dijo Catanzara. “Y entonces tendrías a dos oficiales heridos en las escaleras si los delincuentes todavía estuvieran en el apartamento”.</p><p>Baker les dijo a los investigadores que pensó que estaba en un “embudo fatal” cuando vio a un hombre apuntándole con un rifle después de entrar en el apartamento, utilizando un término táctico para un espacio confinado y peligroso.</p><p>“Pensé que iba a morir en esa entrada porque la acción, como se nos enseña, la acción vence a la reacción”, dijo. “Me lancé a un lado y fue cuando escuché un estallido”.</p><p>Las autoridades arrestaron y acusaron a un hombre de apuntar con un arma hacia Baker una vez que él estaba dentro del apartamento, un par de días después del tiroteo. Un segundo hombre, el que Baker y Rivera habían perseguido dentro del apartamento, fue arrestado y acusado unas dos semanas después.</p><p>Una jueza inicialmente prohibió la divulgación de cualquier material relacionado con los dos casos criminales o el caso administrativo abierto contra Baker después del tiroteo. La orden impidió que COPA publicara videos dentro de los 60 días siguientes al tiroteo, lo cual está requerido por ordenanza de la ciudad.</p><p>El editor de Illinois Answers Project, el Sun-Times y otras organizaciones de noticias solicitaron a la jueza anular su orden el verano pasado. Ella rechazó, pero una corte de apelaciones anuló su orden en marzo por “abuso de discreción”.</p><h3><b>No se han publicado más videos </b></h3><p>COPA finalmente sólo publicó dos videos relacionados con la muerte de Rivera. Los registros muestran que la agencia tiene al menos una docena más en su archivo.</p><p>En la muerte a tiros de Adam Toledo, la agencia publicó 23 archivos de video de cámaras corporales. En la muerte a tiros de Dexter Reed, la agencia publicó 17 de esos archivos, incluyendo de los oficiales que llegaron después del tiroteo.</p><p>Antonio Romanucci, el abogado de la familia de Rivera, dijo que los videos publicados el viernes eran parte de una “narrativa curada destinada a inventar una verdad falsa”.</p><p>“Sabemos que hay una cantidad considerable de metraje de cámara corporal en los momentos inmediatos después de que Krystal fue herida que no ha sido liberada”, dijo Romanucci en un comunicado escrito.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div </div><p>“COPA y el Departamento de Policía de Chicago no deberían seleccionar sólo las partes de audio y video que creen relevantes para perfeccionar una historia encubridora”.</p><p>Pero Shannon Hayes, jefa adjunta de investigaciones de COPA, dijo que la agencia no retuvo videos que normalmente habría liberado. “Determiné qué se relaciona con el uso de la fuerza”, comentó.</p><p>Hayes declaró que la agencia mira “todos los videos y determina qué se relaciona con el incidente que estamos investigando”.</p><p>“Ese es el lenguaje de la política de liberación de videos: los materiales relacionados con el incidente”, dijo. “La consistencia en el proceso no significa necesariamente consistencia en el resultado”.</p><h3><b>Un pasado problemático </b></h3><p>Baker había sido objeto de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >más de una docena de quejas por mala conducta</a> para cuando las autoridades dicen que le disparó e hirió fatalmente a Rivera al confrontar a los dos hombres armados dentro de un apartamento lleno de armas y drogas.</p><p>Baker acumuló cinco de esas quejas como oficial en periodo de prueba, cuando podría haber sido despedido sumariamente porque tenía pocas protecciones sindicales.</p><p>Durante ese tiempo, Baker fue acusado de apuntar con un arma a una mujer que había conocido en línea mientras ella estaba en una cita con otro hombre en un bar del lado norte. La mujer luego se negó a cooperar con los investigadores y Baker no enfrentó ninguna acción disciplinaria en ese caso, muestran los registros.</p><p>Baker solicitó unirse al equipo táctico del Distrito de Gresham en marzo de 2024, pero <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/01/15/chicago-police-department-carlos-baker-krystal-rivera-gresham-district-jon-hein" target="_blank" >el jefe de patrulla Jon Hein bloqueó el movimiento</a>, citando el historial disciplinario de Baker, según un memorando interno.</p><p>Después de presentar una segunda solicitud, en enero de 2025, Baker obtuvo un lugar en el equipo táctico, una posición codiciada y competitiva que a menudo se utiliza como un trampolín para promociones.</p><p>Entre las dos solicitudes, Baker se metió en más problemas.</p><p>No activó sus luces ni sirenas mientras perseguía un auto robado en junio de 2024, que terminó volando por los aires y chocando con otros seis vehículos, muestran los registros. Baker luego <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/02/19/chicago-police-department-krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-gresham-district-misconduct-discipline-investigation-cpd" target="_blank" >disparó accidentalmente su Taser</a> mientras perseguía al conductor sobre una cerca. Se le descontaron dos días de salario por el accidente.</p><p>Cada vez que solicitó unirse al equipo, tuvo el respaldo de su comandante de distrito, Michael Tate. Tate ha sido promovido desde entonces a subinspector de calle, un puesto de alto rango responsable de responder y comandar la escena en eventos importantes en toda la ciudad.</p><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>‘Mi vida está arruinada’ </b></h3><p>COPA no ha anunciado sus hallazgos en la investigación sobre la muerte de Rivera.</p><p>En un momento, Baker le dijo a los investigadores que se sentía “manipulado y mentalmente agotado” al tener que volver a ver las imágenes de la cámara corporal para justificar sus acciones esa noche.</p><p>Baker les dijo a los investigadores que tenía dificultades para vivir en Chicago y para salir tras el tiroteo.</p><p>“No hice nada malo. Y todo el mundo me hace parecer un monstruo... No puedo salir. No puedo hacer nada. No puedo hablar con chicas porque cada mujer piensa que soy el monstruo de este tiroteo”, dijo.</p><p>“Eso es lo que duele”, agregó. “Mi vida está arruinada”.</p><p>Baker dijo que no tenía “tiempo para prestar ayuda” a Rivera cuando se le preguntó sobre su decisión de arrastrarla escaleras abajo.</p><p>“No iba a funcionar”, dijo. “Sabía que ella tenía que llegar a un centro de trauma de Nivel 1 para cirugía, de forma inmediata”.</p><p>En otra entrevista, los investigadores de COPA se centraron en su decisión de subir las escaleras lejos de donde provenían los disparos. Dijo que se estaba protegiendo de lo que pensó que era “la línea de fuego, donde el rifle estaba inicialmente apuntando hacia mí”.</p><p>Baker dijo que se dio cuenta de que Rivera estaba herida cuando la escuchó luchar por respirar.</p><p>“Y, en tu estimación, ¿cuánto tiempo permaneciste en la parte superior de las escaleras?” preguntó un investigador.</p><p>“Toda la noche se sintió como segundos”, dijo. “Todo se sintió como si hubiera pasado en un segundo. Así que no recuerdo. No lo sé”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: No one injured in Austin fire that spread to multiple homes
entries.summary: <p>A fire in the Austin neighborhood tore through three homes Monday morning and displaced over 20 people, according to the Chicago Fire Department. </p><p>Firefighters responded to the extra-alarm call around 9 a.m. in the 4700 block of West Fulton Street, around the corner from the Cicero Avenue Green Line stop. No injuries were reported. </p><p>The fire started in one home and spread to two others, according to the fire department. The flames were extinguished just before noon. </p><p>The cause is under investigation. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">2-11 Alarm on the 4700 block of W Fulton has exposure to both sides; crews are making progress; there are currently no injuries or transports. No further info at this time. <a href="https://t.co/Tm7Lrpak7K">pic.twitter.com/Tm7Lrpak7K</a></p>— Chicago Fire Media (@CFDMedia) <a href="https://twitter.com/CFDMedia/status/2046240305196740944?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 20, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Bears QB Caleb Williams: I'll probably talk 'Iceman' with George Gervin soon
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams plans to talk to Naismith Basketball Hall of Famer George Gervin at some point about their dueling trademark applications for the nickname “Iceman,” but admitted that the entire issue isn’t a top priority. He spoke at Halas Hall on Monday, the first day of the Bears’ offseason program.</p><p>“We’ll probably speak, like I said, at some point,” he said. “Haven’t spoken yet. Have been running around, been busy … We’ll figure it out here soon.”</p><p>In March, Williams filed for copyright protection for his “Iceman” nickname on sports apparel and other items. Four days later, Gervin, a 12-time All-Star and Spurs legend who was nicknamed the “Iceman” in 1973, filed his own trademark application. Gervin told the Sun-Times shortly after that, when it came to sports, he was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/26/bears-caleb-williams-george-gervin-iceman-trademark-nickname-already-taken-nfl-quarterback-aba-nba-basketball-san-antonio-spurs" target="_blank" >“really the ‘Iceman.’</a>”</p><p>Williams said he found the public reaction to the nickname issue funny but referred to the issue as “an open-ended thing” that he was still trying to figure out. “Not the biggest focus right now for me,” he said. “It’s around.”<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: ICE dice que el 81% de arrestados en Operativo Midway Blitz no tenían condenas, insiste no arrestó ciudadanos
entries.summary: <p>Aproximadamente el 81% de las personas arrestadas en el "Operativo Midway Blitz" de Chicago no tenían condenas criminales, a pesar de que los funcionarios federales de inmigración afirmaron repetidamente que el agresivo operativo estaba destinado a atacar a "los peores de los peores".</p><p>Así lo indican las respuestas dadas al senador Dick Durbin y a la delegación congresional de Illinois el 13 de abril, información enviada casi siete meses después de su solicitud en el auge del Operativo Midway Blitz en septiembre. Esto ocurre mientras Todd Lyons, director interino del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) presentó su renuncia el jueves, efectiva el 31 de mayo.</p><p>Esto marca el primer número concreto de arrestos que los funcionarios federales rechazaron anteriormente proporcionar.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-750000" name="module-750000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>"El enfoque del Operativo Midway Blitz fue arrestar a los peores criminales extranjeros y proteger la paz y la dignidad de las comunidades de Illinois", escribió Lyons en la carta del 13 de abril. "Como resultado de los esfuerzos de los valientes hombres y mujeres de ICE, Illinois es más seguro que antes del Operativo Midway Blitz".</p><p>A pesar de esa afirmación, Lyons escribió que entre el 5 de septiembre y el 17 de febrero, ICE realizó 4,570 "arrestos administrativos" en Illinois y dijo que ninguno de esos arrestos fueron ciudadanos estadounidenses. De esos arrestos, una persona fue identificada como beneficiaria de DACA (Acción Diferida para los Llegados en la Infancia).</p><p>Al preguntarle cuántas de las personas arrestadas no tenían condenas penales, sin relación con su estatus migratorio, Lyons escribió que aproximadamente 3,739 no tenían condenas penales y que tenían cargos penales pendientes o eran "infractores de inmigración". Nueve de esos arrestos estaban sujetos a una remoción acelerada.</p><p>Los agentes federales apuntaron al área de Chicago durante el Operativo Midway Blitz entre mediados de septiembre y mediados de octubre. El 12 de septiembre, a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/18/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-asesinado-por-agente-de-ice-en-area-de-chicago-tenia-cocaina-en-el-sistema-autopsia" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González,</a> un padre y cocinero originario de México, le dispararon fatalmente "a quemarropa" mientras supuestamente intentaba huir de un control de tráfico. El Operativo Midway Blitz se ralentizó a mediados de noviembre.</p><p>En la carta, Lyons sostiene que ningún ciudadano estadounidense fue arrestado por oficiales federales de inmigración durante el Operativo Midway Blitz, a pesar de los informes documentados de ciudadanos siendo arrestados.</p><p>Según ProPublica, al menos 170 ciudadanos fueron detenidos en los primeros nueve meses de la administración de Trump, y para mediados de octubre, alrededor de dos docenas de ciudadanos estadounidenses habían sido retenidos por más de un día sin poder contactar a nadie.</p><p>El personal de Durbin también ha documentado la detención reportada de al menos 40 ciudadanos en Illinois entre finales de agosto y principios de noviembre de 2025.</p><p>La carta también dice que el Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) utilizó varias otras agencias federales para ayudar con la aplicación de la ley de inmigración, incluyendo personal del Buró Federal de Investigaciones (FBI), la Administración de Control de Drogas (DEA), la Oficina de Alcohol, Tabaco, Armas de Fuego y Explosivos (ATF), la Oficina Federal de Prisiones, el Servicio de Alguaciles (USMS) y el Servicio Diplomático del Departamento de Estado. Incluso empleados del Servicio de Impuestos Internos (IRS) "ejercieron su autoridad migratoria debidamente delegada y/o asistieron a los oficiales y agentes especiales de ICE con operativos y acciones migratorias", escribió Lyons.</p><p>DHS no llevó un registro de cuántos oficiales de otras agencias trabajaron con ICE, dice la carta. Dentro del DHS, 209 agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza (CBP) trabajaron con sus contrapartes de ICE, y la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA) proporcionó a ICE aproximadamente 100 marshals aéreos federales de manera rotativa. Dentro de DHS, la TSA tiene un acuerdo reembolsable con el apoyo aéreo de ICE por hasta $80 millones.</p><p>Con respecto a las políticas de uso de la fuerza, Lyons escribió que los empleados de DHS siguen las directrices en su manual de empleados y escribió que "DHS no tiene supervisión y no dirige cómo sus socios en la aplicación de la ley federal realizan sus funciones".</p><p>"Todos los oficiales de ICE son capacitados en el Continuo de Uso de la Fuerza de ICE, que incluye formación en desescalada", escribió Lyons. "Además, se les enseña a los oficiales de ICE cómo aplicar el estándar de razonabilidad al emplear la fuerza. Los oficiales de ICE pueden utilizar solo la cantidad de fuerza que sea objetivamente razonable para controlar a un sujeto y el uso de cualquier nivel de fuerza debe cesar de inmediato cuando el sujeto ya no presente una amenaza".</p><p>Al preguntarle sobre los influencers de alto perfil en redes sociales que acompañaron a algunos de los operativos con objetivo más agresivos, Lyons dijo que esos influencers recibieron aprobación previa y firmaron una renuncia acordando cumplir con todas las directrices de ICE para participar en un acompañamiento. Se negaron a proporcionar una lista de qué influencers recibieron acceso.</p><p>La representante Delia Ramírez, demócrata por Illinois, criticó duramente la carta, diciendo al Sun-Times en un comunicado que "cada fascista en la administración responsable del terror del Midway Blitz debe rendir cuentas.</p><p>"Hoy tenemos los datos para probar lo que nuestras comunidades siempre supieron: DHS es la mayor amenaza a nuestra seguridad colectiva, y financiarlo sólo alimenta nuestra propia destrucción y sufrimiento humano", dijo Ramírez.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Bears picking up Darnell Wright's option for 2027
entries.summary: <p>As expected, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> will pick up right tackle Darnell Wright's fifth-year option for 2027, a source confirmed Monday.</p><p>Because Wright was a first-round pick, the Bears had until May 1 to decide whether to extend a fifth-year option to him for the 2027 season. Wright is expected to make $19.07 million that year before becoming a free agent — unless he and the Bears negotiate a long-term deal. That is one of the items on general manager Ryan Poles' agenda before the first game of the 2026 season. </p><p>Poles is scheduled to conduct his annual pre-draft media session Tuesday.</p><p>Keeping Wright was a no-brainer. Since Poles made him the 10th overall pick in 2023, Wright has played all but two games at right tackle, starting in all of them. He was a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/01/10/bears-s-kevin-byard-lg-joe-thuney-selected-as-all-pros-rt-darnell-wright-earns-second-team-pick" target="_blank" >second-team all-pro last year</a>. The Bears have not been tempted to move him to the left side, though — Poles said during the NFL Scouting Combine that the Bears preferred he play next to right guard Jonah Jackson.</p><p>The Bears view Wright as an integral cog in an offensive line that went from one of the NFL's worst in 2024 to one of its best last year, with guard Joe Thuney and since-retired center Drew Dalman each earning Pro Bowl nods. Pro Football Focus graded Wright as the NFL's 15th-best tackle last year. Only six NFL tackles played more snaps in 2025.</p><p></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz. Many were accused of assaulting or resisting federal agents or officers.</p><p>One defendant has pleaded guilty. Twenty have been cleared. Grand jurors refused to indict at least three of the 20, and another member of that group was found not guilty at trial.</p><p>Meanwhile, prosecutors have entered into deferred-prosecution agreements with four additional defendants. In each of those cases, if that person abides by certain terms over a set period of time, the feds have agreed to drop the charges in that case as well.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times is tracking all of the known criminal cases tied to Operation Midway Blitz:</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6e0000" name="html-embed-module-6e0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-table" data-src="visualisation/27349648"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/27349648/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="table visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><div class="PageListO" data-module data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-bb0000" name="list-bb0000"></a> <div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> Latest from Jon Seidel </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListO-items"> <div class="PageListO-items-row"> <div class="PageListO-items-column" data-with-borders> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2026/04/20/trump-chicago-national-guard-midway-blitz-crime" >Judge tosses lawsuit over Trump's push to deploy National Guard to Chicago after his Supreme Court loss</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-gangs/2026/04/20/labar-broman-spann-chicago-gang-crime-four-corner-hustlers" >Chicago gang boss claims he had 'nothing to do with the Four Corner Hustlers' as judge sets life sentence</a> </div> <div class="PageListO-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/16/terrorism-isis-islamic-state-crime-chicago" >Chicago man gets 25 years for serving as a 'press person' for the ISIS terrorist organization</a> </div> </div> </div> </div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: The Onion launches new bid to take over Alex Jones' Infowars and turn it into a parody platform
entries.summary: <p>The satirical news outlet The Onion is back with a new plan to take over the Infowars platforms of conspiracy theorist Alex Jones as his company faces liquidation over more than $1 billion in defamation judgments owed to relatives of victims of the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting.</p><p>Under a proposal submitted Monday to a state judge in Texas, The Onion would be granted an exclusive, temporary license to the intellectual property of Infowars' parent company, Free Speech Systems, allowing the outlet to put its own content on the Infowars website and social media accounts.</p><p>Ben Collins, CEO of Chicago-based The Onion, said the deal could be in place around April 30, if approved by Judge Maya Guerra Gamble in Austin. He said The Onion has already hired people to run Infowars as a parody site including Tim Heidecker, one half of the comedy duo Tim and Eric known for their work on the Cartoon Network’s “Adult Swim” shows.</p><p>“We’ll build this into a bigger comedy network,” Collins said, adding the Sandy Hook families would receive profits from the new operations.</p><p>“A big part of it for us is that the way people consume news now is they see somebody who has no idea what the (expletive) they’re talking about staring into their camera and just like coming up with conspiracy theories or telling you health hacks that will actually get you poisoned, things like that," he said. "We’re going to create a bunch of characters and worlds around those kinds of things."</p><p>After the 2012 Sandy Hook shooting, which killed 20 first graders and six educators in Newtown, Connecticut, Jones called it a hoax staged by “crisis actors” in an effort to increase gun control. Many relatives of the victims, along with an FBI agent who responded to the shooting, sued Jones and his company for defamation and infliction of emotional distress.</p><p>On his show Monday, Jones vowed to fight the licensing proposal in court but acknowledged he and his crew could be kicked out of the building at the end of the month. He said he would continue his shows in another studio he is preparing, and they would air on his personal X account and other new social media accounts and websites, as well as dozens of radio stations. He also has set up new websites for the merchandise he sells, including dietary supplements and clothing that bring in millions of dollars a year.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Phillip/AP file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I’m going to continue the exact same show," he said. "It’ll just be called the ‘Alex Jones Show.’ So, it’s the same satellite, same system. It's a different news site and news studio. So I’m not going anywhere.”</p><p>The licensing deal with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/05/10/onion-chicago-owner-global-tetrahedron-satire-news" >The Onion</a> would be for six months, with the right to renew it for another six months as a court-appointed receiver works to eventually sell the assets of Infowars’ parent company, Austin-based Free Speech Systems, and give proceeds to the Sandy Hook families. The receiver is supporting the plan, which calls for The Onion to pay $81,000 a month to cover the rent for the building housing Infowars' studios, along with utilities and other costs.</p><p>During a trial of the defamation suit in Connecticut in 2022, victims' relatives testified that people whom they called followers of Jones subjected them to death and rape threats, in-person harassment and abusive comments on social media over the hoax claims. Jones argued there was never any proof that linked him to the actions of others.</p><p>A jury and judge awarded the families and the FBI agent more than $1.4 billion in damages. In a similar lawsuit in Texas, the parents of a child killed at Sandy Hook were awarded nearly $50 million. Jones appealed both awards. He lost his challenges to the Connecticut judgment, while his appeal of the Texas award is still pending.</p><p>Jones filed for bankruptcy in late 2022. In those proceedings, an auction was held in November 2024 to liquidate Infowars’ assets to help pay the defamation judgments, and The Onion was named the winning bidder. But the bankruptcy judge threw out the auction results, citing problems with the process and The Onion’s bid.</p><p>The attempt to sell off Infowars’ assets later moved to the state court in Texas, where Guerra Gamble appointed a receiver to liquidate the assets of Jones' company. Jones is also appealing that ruling, which has put a hold on the liquidation.</p><p>A lawyer for the Sandy Hook families who sued Jones in Connecticut said they support The Onion's plan.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/05/10/onion-chicago-owner-global-tetrahedron-satire-news" >New Chicago-based company Global Tetrahedron wants to make The Onion great again</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

entries.title: Tim Cook will step down as Apple CEO
entries.summary: <p>Apple CEO Tim Cook is stepping down from the job that he inherited from the late Steve Jobs, ending a nearly 15-year reign that saw the company’s market value soar by more than $3.6 trillion during an iPhone-fueled era of prosperity.</p><p>Cook, 65, will turn the CEO duties over to Apple’s head of hardware engineering, John Ternus, on Sept. 1 while remaining involved with the Cupertino, California, company as executive chairman. That’s similar to the transitions made by Amazon’s Jeff Bezos and Netflix’s Reed Hastings after they ended their highly successful tenures as CEO.</p><p>“It has been the greatest privilege of my life to be the CEO of Apple and to have been trusted to lead such an extraordinary company," Cook said in a statement. “I love Apple with all of my being, and I am so grateful to have had the opportunity to work with a team of such ingenious, innovative, creative, and deeply caring people.”</p><p>Ternus, 50, has been with Apple for the past quarter century, including the past five years overseeing the engineering underlying the iPhone, iPad and Mac — a role that made him a prime candidate to succeed Cook.</p><p>“I am profoundly grateful for this opportunity to carry Apple’s mission forward,” Ternus said in a statement.</p><p>The transition to a new CEO comes at a pivotal time for Apple. Artificial intelligence has unleashed the most upheaval within the industry since Jobs unveiled the first iPhone in 2007. Apple has gotten off to a rough start in AI after stumbling in its efforts to deliver new features built on the technology, as promised nearly two years ago.</p><p>Earlier this year, Apple finally turned to Google — an early leader in the AI race — for help making the iPhone's virtual assistant Siri into a more conversational and versatile helper.</p><p>Although he never shook the perception that he lacked Jobs’ vision, Cook leveraged the popularity of the iPhone and other breakthroughs orchestrated by his predecessor to lift Apple to heights that seemed unfathomable when it was on the brink of bankruptcy during the mid-1990s.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Whole Foods grocery stores coming to Wicker Park, Lake View
entries.summary: <p>Whole Foods is planning to open grocery stores in the Wicker Park and Lake View neighborhoods. </p><p>The Wicker Park location is set for 1200 N. Ashland Ave., the site of a former CVS at the intersections of Division Street and Ashland and Milwaukee avenues. A spokesperson for Amazon, which owns Whole Foods, confirmed it is seeking to open a location there but would not share more detail, including when the store could open.</p><p>The high-end grocery chain is also seeking to open a store at 827 W. Belmont Ave. in Lake View, <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/share/p/1BnTbXfUvL/" target="_blank" >according to the Lakeview East Chamber of Commerce</a>. An opening date there has not been announced.</p><p>Earlier this year, <a class="Link" href="https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/company-news/amazon-fresh-go-stores-closing-expanding-whole-foods" target="_blank" >Amazon said it plans</a> to open more than 100 new Whole Foods locations over the next few years. The company says it will also shutter its Amazon Go and Amazon Fresh stores and convert some locations into Whole Foods, according to a news release.</p><p>The Wicker Park Whole Foods will be in the landmarked Home Bank and Trust Company Building. A CVS most recently occupied the building's ground floor until it closed in 2023, according to Block Club Chicago, which first reported the planned Whole Foods in Wicker Park.</p><p>Up the road in Logan Square, a Trader Joe's grocery store is set to open in a vacant storefront that used to be a CVS at 2053 N. Milwaukee Ave.</p>

entries.title: Mike Vasil embraces new role as White Sox cheerleader during recovery from Tommy John surgery
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Right-hander Mike Vasil did it all last season for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ pitching staff, serving as a spot starter, long reliever, high-leverage man and closer.</p><p>But Vasil, a former Rule 5 Draft pick, has a new role this season and one he certainly didn’t ask for.</p><p>Clubhouse cheerleader.</p><p>With his second season in the big leagues lost to a catastrophic elbow injury and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/17/white-sox-mike-vasil-to-undergo-season-ending-tommy-john-surgery" target="_blank" ><u>Tommy John surgery</u></a>, Vasil has traveled with the Sox and embraced the little things he can contribute to help his teammates develop while he can’t — not on the field, at least.</p><p>‘‘I just try and bring positive vibes to the guys, some good energy,’’ Vasil, 26, said in an empty clubhouse this month in Kansas City after his teammates headed out for the pregame work he won’t be able to approach for months.</p><p>With his powerful right arm in a brace, it was a rare moment the gregarious Vasil expressed a little melancholy as he stares down his yearlong recovery.</p><p>But there’s no containing the outgoing personality of a player who famously brought two to the Sox, if you count <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/07/12/white-sox-mike-vasil-batman-bullpen-will-venable-rebuild" target="_blank" ><u>the ‘‘Batman’’ persona</u></a> he channeled last season on the mound. (Yes, his ‘‘Batman’’ mask is in tow this season.)</p><p>So while Vasil isn’t throwing, he’s assisting with scouting reports, tallying his teammates’ shutdown innings for in-house competition and looking for any other way to keep spirits high on a rebuilding team.</p><p>‘‘Any way I can contribute while I’m doing my rehab, that’s really what I’m here to do,’’ Vasil said. ‘‘It keeps my mind fresh. It makes it a lot more enjoyable. At the same time, I want to be a part of the team. Winning matters to me. This team matters to me a lot. I care about what we’re doing. It’s not like I’m just a guy rehabbing in the org. I want to be able to do everything I can to help this team win.’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mike Vasil is interviewed after a home win last season.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Geoff Stellfox/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Sox are missing Vasil as much as he misses them. He finished tied with Adrian Houser last season for the lead among Sox pitchers in WAR (2.9, according to Baseball Reference) and trailed only Colson Montgomery’s 3.3 for the whole team. He pitched 101 innings — a major-league high for relievers — across just about every conceivable scenario.</p><p>Vasil’s 2.50 ERA led all rookies. He also had a 5-3 record with four saves in six chances and struck out 82 in 47 appearances, including three starts.</p><p></p><p>Without their Swiss Army knife in the bullpen, Sox relievers have struggled to a 5.64 ERA this season, the third-worst in the majors entering play Monday.</p><p></p><p>General manager Chris Getz has said the Sox are looking to second-year flame-thrower Grant Taylor (3.97 ERA) to cover around 100 innings in a variety of roles — most notably <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>as an opener</u></a> — but Taylor has been knocked around a couple of times in between outings in which he has looked unhittable.</p><p></p><p>Meanwhile, closer Seranthony Dominguez (5.63) has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/17/white-sox-seranthony-dominguez-anthony-kay-athletics" target="_blank" ><u>blown two of his six save opportunities</u></a><u> </u>and made things more interesting than the Sox were hoping when they inked him to a two-year, $20 million deal.</p><p></p><p>And it has been a mixed bag for Jordan Hicks (4.91), Jordan Leasure (5.23) and Sean Newcomb (4.61), prompting well-worn paths among Chicago, Triple-A Charlotte and the waiver wire. The Sox already have cycled through 21 pitchers this season.</p><p></p><p>Manager Will Venable sure would love to be able to send up the ‘‘bat signal,’’ just like Vasil would.</p><p></p><p>‘‘There have definitely been some times throughout the year, which makes it hard, where I could see how I could plug myself in and save some innings for some other guys,’’ Vasil said. ‘‘What’s tough for me is I liked doing that, knowing I could come in and eat a chunk of innings when we needed it. But I think right now it’s still early, and everyone’s really falling into a role. We’re trying different things. Everyone’s flexible.’’</p>

entries.title: Review: ‘Windfall’ heartbreakingly asks if money buys justice
entries.summary: <p>For all of its unblinking, real-world brutality, “Windfall” unspools with an ethereal, dreamlike quality, nightmares included. The drama arrives at Steppenwolf as a commission from Oscar-winner <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/10/windfall-steppenwolf-play-tarell-alvin-mccraney-moonlight-writer" target="_blank" ><u>Tarell Alvin McCraney</u></a><u>.</u></p><p>As he did in the Tony-nominated drama “Choir Boy” and the Oscar-winning screenplay “Moonlight” (based on his unpublished play “In Moonlight Black Boys Look Blue”), “Windfall” lives at the intersection of crushing tragedy and indomitable humanity. Terrible things befall the characters of “Windfall,” but it doesn’t extinguish their music, their fight or their love for each other.</p><p>At the core of “Windfall” is a blunt question: How much money is a murdered Black man worth? And if that money comes from the very system that murdered him, is taking it morally reprehensible or a modicum of justice?</p><p>Director Awoye Timpo creates a world that ripples between the real and the slightly surreal. McCraney embeds all-too familiar violence and economic struggle with a glimmering sheen of the spectral. Or maybe that light is just the flare from a gunshot.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In "Windfall," tyrannical viciousness screams to the surface. But so does a scrim of fantastical protections: ancestral, spiritual and familial. Tinges of the supernatural aside, "Windfall" is deeply rooted in a story that’s been repeated more times than anyone will ever be able to count: This time around, it’s singing while Black that’ll get you shot.</p><p>The six-strong ensemble weaves a powerful story. At the onset, Marcus (Glenn Davis), his nonbinary sibling Eli (Esco Jouléy, alternately tough and vulnerable), their friend Cori (<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2024/10/25/jon-michael-hill-tv-actor-steppenwolf-lucy-and-leroy" target="_blank" ><u>Jon Michael Hill,</u></a> purveyor of much of the show’s humor) and “Brother 1” (Namir Smallwood, a magnetic presence throughout) are camped in front of the kind of black ops site overseen by Chicago cop Jon Burge, who<a class="Link" href="https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/former-chicago-police-officer-jon-burge-sentenced-lying-about-police-torture" target="_blank" ><u> tortured and abused</u></a> more than 100 people over a two-decade career, leading to false confessions and wrongful imprisonment. They’re protesting the very existence of the site, and trying to save a child who has vanished into the building.</p><p>Through song and chant, Marcus, Eli, Brother and Cori heighten awareness and start a movement that snowballs into an avalanche. The cops mow down the encampment. Brother, Cori and Eli make it out. Marcus gets shot. (None of this is a spoiler as it all unfolds early on.)</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.5" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> Windfall </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c70000" name="module-c70000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through May 31<br><b>Where:</b> Steppenwolf Ensemble Theatre, 1650 N. Halsted<br><b>Info:</b> Tickets are $20 - $148.50; run time is two hours, 10 minutes including one intermission. More info at <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org" target="_blank" ><u>Steppenwolf.org</u></a></div> </div><p>The city offers Marcus’ adopted father Henri “Mr. Mano” Tamaño (Michael Potts) a settlement substantial enough to let him keep his house. When Mr. Mano refuses, the plot gestures toward Charles Dickens, but inverted: Mr. Mano has four visitors – some of the flesh, some possibly not — who try to convince him to take the cash.</p><p>The ensemble is strong throughout, but the dynamic between Potts’ Mr. Mano and Davis’ Marcus is particularly charged, their exchanges blistering. When an anguished Mr. Mano screams that he cannot take “blood money,” Marcus’ retort is tragically accurate: All money is blood money.</p><p>Mr. Mano is both sympathetic and stubborn. Appeals to logic, emotion and finally threats will not change his mind. Potts’ Mr. Mano makes the money seem like Judas’ 30 pieces of silver: a betrayal of the absolute worst kind.</p><p>Davis captures the complex, contradictory relationship between Marcus and Mr. Mano. Marcus loves his father deeply, and is prone to screaming at him in frustration. Marcus’ determination to expose torture and free survivors is aspirational and all-too-sadly familiar. Burge’s work continues, not in his name but definitely in his spirit. Black sites, detention centers — whatever you want to call them, they’re part of a law enforcement modus operandi that goes back centuries.</p><p>All of which sounds like “Windfall” is an exercise in grimness, but that it is definitely not. Music (great work by music director Mahmoud Khan) wafts through the story from gossamer lilt to percussive stomp as Marcus, Eli, Cori and Brother send their voices up to the stars, sometimes emphasizing the percussive insistence with drums and bells.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> Ensemble member Alana Arenas plays several characters who visit Michael Potts’ character in “Windfall.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Alana Arenas is invaluable in a trio of unnerving roles, none of whom provide names: First Lady shows up to tell Mr. Mano about the settlement the city is offering for the death of his son. Miss Second is an appraiser (or an apparition — could be either) who informs Mr. Mano his taxes are in arrears and his home is about to be put up for auction. The Last One appears clad all in black like the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come, and with a similarly dire message.</p><p>McCraney wisely doesn’t attempt to sew things together neatly — or at all really — for Mr. Mano. Part of his gift as a playwright is his ability to leave things realistically ambiguous without the play feeling unfinished. “Windfall” provides no answers, but it finds the sustaining power of music and family, with or without life-changing (blood) money.</p><p>There’s power to spare in “Windfall,” but there’s also a looseness to some scenes — the intensity incrementally flags at times, especially during moments of brief audience interaction. And while the music is immersive, it’s not always seamlessly woven into the dialogue. These are minor points — tweaks, not dealbreakers.</p><p>Finally, some context on the settlement: The amount offered to Mr. Mano for the murder of his son is less than 1% of CPD’s 2026 budget. In real life, and after costing the city some $85 million in settlements, Burge retired to Florida with<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2014/7/3/18567894/illinois-supreme-court-ruling-means-burge-can-keep-his-pension" target="_blank" ><u> his full pension. </u></a></p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2026/04/10/windfall-steppenwolf-play-tarell-alvin-mccraney-moonlight-writer" >In ‘Windfall,’ Oscar winner Tarell Alvin McCraney asks if money really buys justice for Black men</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2024/10/31/jon-michael-hill-tv-actor-steppenwolf-lucy-and-leroy" >This is the year of actor Jon Michael Hill, from soulful monologues to playing bluesman Robert Johnson</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2024/03/17/phylicia-rashad-directing-black-family-drama-purpose-at-steppenwolf" >At Steppenwolf Theatre, Phylicia Rashad is reimagining the Black family drama</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
